module.sous_titre: A hundred ways of loving in the Arabic language

eveil.label: Love that awakens
eveil.desc: The first movement of the soul toward the other — inclination, the meeting, nascent intimacy.

chant.label: Love that sings
chant.desc: When love seeks the words, the verses, the voice — tenderness that says itself.

brulure.label: Love that burns
brulure.desc: The passion that consumes, that gnaws, that thins as a bindweed its host.

perte.label: Love that loses itself
perte.desc: Madness, melancholy, wandering — when the lover no longer recognises himself.

retrouvailles.label: Love that finds again
retrouvailles.desc: Union, the bond, fusion — the soothing of the lover in the presence.

divin.label: Divine love
divin.desc: When human love recognises its archetype — the ultimate station of the journey.

feu.label: Fire
feu.desc: Burning, flame, blaze — love as combustion.

eau.label: Water
eau.desc: Thirst, drowning, river — love as a liquid abyss.

corps.label: The sick body
corps.desc: The illness of love, languor, physical wasting.

folie.label: Madness
folie.desc: Loss of reason, wandering, obsession — Majnūn and his descendants.

lien.label: The bond
lien.desc: Union, attachment, fusion — what reunites the lovers.

separation.label: Separation
separation.desc: Estrangement, exile, absence — what sets them apart.

douceur.label: Gentleness
douceur.desc: Tenderness, peace, intimacy — love without violence.

eclat.label: Radiance and the snare
eclat.desc: Beauty, seduction, charm, bewitchment — amorous fascination.

ivresse.label: Intoxication
ivresse.desc: Vertigo, ecstasy, transport — the lover made giddy by the presence.

hubb.description: The word ḥubb is the root-word, the matrix of the whole amorous family in Arabic. Its etymology is the object of countless hypotheses: some see in it the seed of a desert plant, which contains the whole plant in germ; others, the immaculate whiteness of teeth — the gleam of beauty that calls for love; others again, the image of the vessel, which lets itself be filled. Three trails, one and the same mystery. Ḥubb is the love sung by Umm Kulthūm — the love of the wandering poets, of the solitary princesses, of the monks in the desert. A noble and classical feeling, the paradigm of carnal passion and of the torments of youth, it is also the ever-renewed motif of tender contrition, the constantly resumed support of desire. In the Quran, ḥubb founds the relationship between God and the creature: yuḥibbuhum wa yuḥibbūnahu — “He loves them and they love Him” (V, 54). It is this verse that the whole of Sufi mysticism has meditated upon as the key to divine reciprocity.

temoin.fr: He loves them and they love Him

hubb.meditation: What is the simplest word that you no longer dare to say?
hubb.ouverture: The Sufi tradition holds this verse to be the key to the tawḥīd of love: God does not receive love, He gives it first, and it is from His prior love that our love for Him can spring up. To love, says Ibn ʿArabī, is always to answer a call already sent out.

hawa.definition: The sudden inclination, the attraction that carries one away
hawa.description: Hawā names that precise instant when the soul tips over. Before the word, there was nothing — or rather, there was ordinary life, the course of the hours. And then something inclines, abruptly, toward a face, a voice, a silhouette glimpsed. That is hawā: the sudden, violent, passionate attraction of the soul toward its object — a fall, more than a decision. In the very root of the word, one hears hawā, the verb to fall. Love as a fall. One does not rise toward what one loves — one falls into it. The poet ʿAbbās ibn al-Aḥnaf (8th century) made of it the great vocabulary of his court: sulṭān al-hawā, the power of love; ḥukm al-hawā, its law; sirr al-hawā, its secret. His contemporary Sulaymān al-Mustaʿīn wrote this verse that the whole tradition has kept: “Do not blame a king for lowering himself before love, for the humiliation of love is a second royalty.”

temoin.fr: The power of love

hawa.meditation: Toward what do you incline, without having chosen it?
hawa.ouverture: Sufism turns this word over with a fine subtlety: what makes you fall is exactly what can raise you. The hawā that ordinary morality blames — the irrational inclination, the passion that undoes — is also, says Ibn ʿArabī, the organ by which the soul recognises its origin. One always falls downward. But the low, sometimes, is on high.

ilf.definition: Amorous intimacy, the companionship of lovers
ilf.description: Ilf names that particular gentleness which settles between two beings when the first attraction has subsided and the companionship begins. It is no longer the blaze of hawā, nor the ecstasy of waṣl — it is the art of living side by side, of growing accustomed to one another without eroding one another, of weaving a daily intimacy that does not extinguish the flame but keeps it at the hearth. The word comes from the root ʾ-l-f, which means to associate, to assemble, to gather. The same root yields alif — the first letter of the Arabic alphabet, the one that stands upright, straight, and that inaugurates all the nouns. In the mystical symbolism of Daylamī (10th century), alif subdivides into three movements: alifa (to love), ilf (the lover), allafa (the love that unites). A single letter, three ways of being together. It is under this word that Ibn Ḥazm of Andalusia (993-1064) placed his masterpiece, Ṭawq al-ḥamāma fī l-ulfa wa-l-ullāf — The Ring of the Dove, on love and lovers — one of the most beautiful psychologies of love of all time.

temoin.fr: The Ring of the Dove, on love and lovers

ilf.meditation: With whom do you no longer have to put on a show?
ilf.ouverture: The Sufi way recognises in ilf a very high station, more difficult than the blaze. To burn is easy — to last together without betraying one another is less so. The Companions of the Prophet are named al-ulfāʾ, those who gathered around him in daily intimacy — not in ecstasy, but at the shared table.

mouanassa.definition: Intimacy in the presence of the beloved
mouanassa.description: If ilf is amorous habit, muʾānasa is its inner quality: to be in the intimacy of the beloved being, to seek actively his company, to find in his presence the rest that the solitary soul lacks. The word comes from the root uns, which means familiarity, soothing, the contrary of anxious strangeness. It is the same root that yields insān, the human being — as if humanity itself were defined by its capacity to be in presence, by that quality of being which no pure solitude could fill. The thinker Abū Ḥayyān at-Tawḥīdī (10th century) devoted an entire book to the word, al-Imtāʿ wa-l-muʾānasa — On delectation and fair company. The title says it all already: love, at this station, is no longer a fever, it is a shared savour.

temoin.fr: On delectation and fair company

mouanassa.meditation: With whom can you fall silent, without the silence weighing?
mouanassa.ouverture: In the Sufi lexicon, uns is the name of the station in which the servant has ceased to flee the presence of God — in which He is no longer a dreaded instance, but a sought company. It is the exact inverse of waḥsha, the wild fear. Human muʾānasa is therefore a school: to love soothes — or reveals the anxiety that had to be named.

ghazal.description: Before being a poetic genre, ghazal is a gesture: the art of spinning speech as one spins wool — whence the primary sense of the root gh-z-l, to spin, to weave. The amorous poet weaves his verses as one weaves a veil, and it is beneath this veil that the beloved will appear better than in her nakedness. The one who practises taghazzul is strongly drawn to a person and expresses it through the beauty of language. From there, the ghazal becomes the great Arabic and Persian lyric genre: the brief, chiselled love poem, in which each distich is self-sufficient while linking to the following ones. Ḥāfiẓ of Shiraz (14th century) is its absolute master, and in him the human ghazal becomes the transparent veil of a divine ghazal. When flattery passes the bounds of sincerity, Arabic names it khitāl — to distinguish the song that reveals from the seduction that lies.

temoin.fr: Every song of love that is not true is but a deception

ghazal.meditation: When you speak of love — do you weave a veil that reveals, or a veil that lies?
ghazal.ouverture: Sufism made of the ghazal its language par excellence. When Ibn al-Fāriḍ or Ibn ʿArabī sing a beloved, they always sing, beneath the image, the divine face. The poetic veil is not a disguise: it is the only way to look at a Light that would blind, seen face on. Human beauty is, says Ibn ʿArabī, the window of the Real.

hubb-oudri.definition: Courtly love, the pure love of the Banū ʿUdhrah
hubb-oudri.description: One names ḥubb ʿudhrī the love that is founded, paradoxically, on the refusal of union. Its theoretical inventor is Ibn Dāwūd al-Iṣbahānī (868-910), a jurist and poet whom the tradition nicknames “the Boileau of the Arabs,” in his Kitāb az-Zahra — The Book of the Flower. According to him, true love rests on three pillars: distance (ʿazl), pious continence (warāʿ, ʿiffa), and accepted separation. The word comes from the Banū ʿUdhrah, a legendary Yemeni tribe where it was said: we die when we love. It is in this tradition that the figure of Majnūn is born — Qays ibn al-Mulawwaḥ, become mad for Laylā to the point that separation does not distance him from her, but engraves her in him forever. Union, in this love, is not refused by accident: it is avoided as a contamination. To love is to keep the lack alive. This love is also called al-hawā al-ʿudhrī. The Occitan troubadour, later, would be its direct heir, by the Arab-Andalusian ways.

temoin.fr:
I pass before the house, the house of Laylā,
I kiss this wall, I embrace that one.
Is it from loving the walls that I lose my reason?
— No, my heart. It is from loving the people who dwell within.

hubb-oudri.meditation: What would you love — if you accepted never to have it?
hubb-oudri.ouverture: The Sufi way takes up exactly this intuition: the true Beloved is not graspable. Every grasp is a veil laid over the Presence. And the veil, says Ibn ʿArabī, is also the appearing. The ʿudhrī learns in human love what the Sufi learns in divine love — that separation is not absence, but the other name of the meeting.

mahabba.description: Maḥabba shares its root with ḥubb, but denotes something more clear-cut, more white, more lasting. An ancient etymology links the word to ṣafāʾ — purity, whiteness — as if maḥabba were love decanted, rid of its storms. In the vocabulary of mysticism, al-maḥabba is addressed essentially to God. It is the passion of the one who chants the Quran without being able to stop; it is the flame of the mystic who, through the dhikr, remembers God until the remembering and the remembered merge. What seems a peaceful friendship in reality conceals, says Junayd, the boundless swell of the heart toward the Beloved — and the torment that accompanies it. The first great Sufi masters — Rābiʿa, Ḥallāj, Junayd — made of maḥabba the first station of the spiritual path: before knowledge, before practice, love.

temoin.fr: He has made faith dear to you and adorned it in your hearts

mahabba.meditation: What, within you, has been made dear without your having chosen it?
mahabba.ouverture: Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya (8th century), the first great mystic of Islam, said: “My God, if I worship You out of fear of hell, burn me in hell; if I worship You out of hope of paradise, exclude me from it. But if I worship You for Your own sake, do not deprive me of Your beauty.” There is maḥabba in its pure state: a love that no longer calculates.

hanane.description: Ḥanān names tenderness in the most precise sense of the word — the quality of what inclines toward the other with gentleness, without violence, without demand. It is the affection of a mother for her child, but also that of the beloved, of the wife, of the friend. Al-ḥanīna denotes the person who feels this tenderness, al-ḥannān the one who radiates it. The root ḥ-n-n has a vast reach: it is also the nostalgic groaning of the camel that seeks its herd, the sigh of the soul that remembers. Thus ḥanīn names at once tenderness and nostalgia — as if the two were but a single movement of the heart, toward what is dear, toward what is lacking. In the Quran, al-Ḥannān is one of the divine Names: the One who inclines tenderly toward His creature. It is the attribute that answers al-Mannān, the Benefactor. Tenderness is not, in Islam, a feminine or weak feeling — it is a Name of God.

temoin.fr: And tenderness from Us

hanane.meditation: Toward whom do you incline, without even deciding it?
hanane.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish three movements of the heart's inclination: hawā, the passionate fall; ʿishq, burning love; ḥanān, stable tenderness. Only the third lasts. It is the one that, after the storms of the path, remains when all the rest has been consumed — the very quality of the soul that has arrived.

sabbaba.definition: Tender love, the love that pours itself out in words
sabbaba.description: Ṣabāba comes from the root ṣ-b-b, which means to pour, to make flow. It is the love that pours itself out — not in cries nor in impulses, but in tender words, in patient letters, in murmured confidences. The lover who feels this sentiment pours his heart into language, word after word, without overflowing. The word applies to desire when it combines two qualities often opposed: fineness and strength. A man who feels a burning passion for a woman, but who expresses it only through careful letters, chiselled missives, is called rajul ṣabbūn — a delicate man, though deeply in love. The Arabic tradition reads this restraint not as a weakness but as the mark of a mature love, which knows that there is more in the slow word than in the rapid gesture. The ṣabāba is cousin to ḥanān and to ḥubb al-ʿafīf — all three form the family of the chaste and long-spoken loves.

temoin.fr: The ṣabāba is nothing other than a fire kindled in the entrails

sabbaba.meditation: What word have you held back, that would have said love better than a gesture?
sabbaba.ouverture: In the Sufi way, amorous writing is a discipline. Ibn ʿArabī wrote to his beloved Niẓām poems that his detractors wanted to forbid, until he composed the mystical commentary upon them himself — to show that beneath each human image was hidden a movement of the soul toward the Real. The written tongue slows the heart, and the slowed heart sees better.

mawadda.definition: Love-affection that endures, love become spiritual
mawadda.description: Mawadda is wadd (love-affection) when it takes on a character of eternity and of spirituality. It is no longer the simple inclination of the heart, but authentic love-friendship, the stable bond that has crossed the seasons and that has settled. The word covers, in the Sufi lexicon, sensibly the same field as maḥabba — with this nuance, that it insists more on reciprocity and on the visible of the feeling. The Quran uses it to describe what must reign between spouses: “And among His signs, He has created for you, from yourselves, spouses that you may find rest in them, and He has placed between you mawadda and raḥma” (XXX, 21). These three words — sukūn (rest), mawadda (lasting affection), raḥma (mercy) — draw the Quranic ideal of the couple. One also says miwadd, al-miwadd. The family of the word gathers: wadd, pure love; widād, its lexical definition; wadūd, the aptitude to love without counting — one of the 99 divine Names.

temoin.fr: He has placed between you affection and mercy

mawadda.meditation: What love, within you, has become spiritual — without your having decided it?
mawadda.ouverture: The passage from ḥubb to mawadda is one of the alchemical transformations that the Sufis describe. The love that burns calms into a gentle light; the love that demands becomes the love that remains. It is not a diminishment — it is an elevation. The fire is not dead: it has become warmth.

widad.definition: Love, according to the classical definition
widad.description: Widād is, in the Arabic lexicographical tradition, the very definition of love. It is by this word that the great dictionary Qāmūs al-muḥīṭ of al-Fayrūzābādī (15th century) — one of the monuments of the language, of considerable reputation among the lovers of Arabic — chooses to define the amorous feeling. As if, to explain ḥubb to a cultivated reader, one had to resort to a word still more ancient, still more simple: widād. The word shares its root with wadd and mawadda, and names love in what it has of the most essential: the affectionate inclination, the leaning that unites two beings without noise. Where ḥubb can be tumultuous, widād is soothed; where ʿishq burns, widād radiates quietly. In Andalusian poetry, al-widād is often the word that closes a poem — as if, after having sought it through all the faces of passion, one returned to it to conclude: this is what it was.

temoin.fr: Widād is the limpidity of the heart

widad.meditation: What limpidity, within you, no longer lets itself be troubled by the seasons?
widad.ouverture: When a mystic speaks of his bond with God in terms of widād, he indicates a station in which passion has calmed without losing anything of its intensity. The heart no longer desires violently — it is what it desired. It is what the way names maqām al-qurb, the station of nearness: one no longer loves from afar, one has entered into what one loved.

wadd.description: Wudd names pure love — elevated, marked with grandeur, rid of anxiety. It is not a feverish passion; it is a noble affection, almost chivalric, which likens human love to divine generosity. This is why, in the mystical lexicon, the word holds a place of choice: it makes the bridge between the tenderness of the creatures and the sovereign benevolence of God. The Quran employs it alongside its intensive wadūd (XI, 90; LXXXV, 14) — the Loving, one of the 99 divine Names. This root w-d-d thus runs through the whole lexicon of love, from the simple inclination to the supreme attribute of the Divinity. To love with wudd is to love as God loves: without calculation, without fear of lacking, in overabundance. The word is in favour throughout the mystical lexicon, and several masters have employed it to describe the state of the heart that has surpassed the trials of passion and that henceforth radiates a stable love.

temoin.fr: My Lord is most Merciful, most Loving

wadd.meditation: What generosity within you remains intact — even when it is not returned to you?
wadd.ouverture: The Sufi way distinguishes two movements: to receive the love of God, and to become His loving in the world. Wudd belongs to the second movement: it is the love that no longer expects anything, because it has understood that it was itself the answer to the call that preceded it. To love thus, says Ḥallāj, is to cease being the lover in order to become love.

ichq.description: ʿIshq is the densest, the most charged word of the Arabic amorous lexicon. Its etymology is revealing: it derives from ʿashaqa, the bindweed — that herbaceous plant with a twining stem, of the family of the convolvulaceae, which wraps itself around the tree and can no longer detach itself from it. The lover is called ʿāshiq; his passion wraps him, thins him, exhausts him as the bindweed its host. ʿIshq shadīd: violent desire. The domain of the word oscillates between the simple aspiration to find the beloved person again and a lasciviousness that can stray into excess. The concept is so central that an entire dīwān of the great Ḥāfiẓ of Shiraz (14th century) bears the title Love, the lover, the beloved — that is, in Persian, ʿishq-o ʿāsheq-o maʿshūq: three words, a single root, a single movement. In Sufism, ʿishq becomes the name of the burning mystical desire — the one that consumes Majnūn for Laylā, that consumes Rūmī for Shams, and that, at a certain degree, always overflows the language one lends it.

temoin.fr:
Come, cupbearer, send round the cup and hand it to me —
love appeared easy at first, but what torments it has been necessary to traverse
temoin.source: Ḥāfiẓ, first ghazal of the Dīwān

ichq.meditation: What bindweed of desire wraps you — and thins you, until you no longer know what of you still stands upright?
ichq.ouverture: The orthodox theologians long refused to apply ʿishq to the relationship between God and the human being — too carnal, too intense, too dangerous. But Ḥallāj then Aḥmad Ghazālī (the mystical brother of the great al-Ghazālī) forced this door: ʿishq became the name of divine love par excellence, the one in which the lover wraps himself around the Real and accepts to be consumed by it. What gnaws you can become what lightens you.

taachchaqa.definition: To fall into the snares of love, to become infatuated
taachchaqa.description: Taʿashshaqa is the verb that says to fall in love — but with that sense proper to ʿishq: to let oneself be caught, like a bird in a net or a traveller in a climbing plant. The word evokes the rapidity, the ease with which the soul yields to attraction. Al-ʿashshāq is the one who loves very quickly and easily; in the feminine, ʿashshāqa. Mutanabbī (915-965), perhaps the greatest Arab poet, made of it one of his famous verses: he marvelled, it is said, to see how well the one who desires nothing knows how to die. The casualness of taʿashshaqa covers, for him, a grave question: where does the soul go that gives itself so easily? And the soul that does not give itself, is it alive? The word is in close kinship with futūn, bewitchment, and with ladgh, the bite. But where these two words insist on the wound, taʿashshaqa keeps something light — it is the love that does not yet take itself seriously.

temoin.fr:
I wonder, astonished, how the one who desires nothing
knows how to die

taachchaqa.meditation: What does the ease with which you yield — or with which you refuse — teach you?
taachchaqa.ouverture: The Sufi way does not condemn taʿashshaqa: it educates it. The soul that becomes infatuated quickly is also the one that can, quickly, recognise God behind the face that has captured it. Spiritual education then consists in letting the inclination go to its term: to rise from the beloved to the sign, from the sign to the Presence.

chaaf.definition: The burning of love, the ardour of feeling
chaaf.description: Shaʿaf is the precise burning of amorous feeling — the instant when love reaches the heart and disturbs it. When one says of someone shaʿafahu l-ḥubb, one says that he has made himself ill by reason of the fire of love that devours him. The idea is that of a powerlessness before the trial of affection: one lets oneself be consumed by it. The root sh-ʿ-f evokes the summit, the highest — as if the burning of love reached precisely the culminating point of the heart, its most sensitive organ. The word is not to be confused with its cousin shaghaf (love-overflowing), although the two are sometimes used one for the other. The shaʿaf is more precise: it is the heat that touches, whereas shaghaf is the flood that submerges. In Persian, the equivalent exists: del-sukhté, “burned heart” — whose echo runs through all classical Iranian poetry, from ʿAṭṭār to Saʿdī.

temoin.fr: He has burned his heart with love

chaaf.meditation: To what summit of yourself has the burning arrived — and what has it changed there?
chaaf.ouverture: The Sufis read the shaʿaf as a spiritual warning: all that burns you is the trial of your attachment. One single thing deserves to burn you entirely — the Presence. All the other flames are trials that the soul inflicts upon itself in order to recognise, by contrast, the one it was awaiting without knowing it.

chaghaf.description: Shaghaf names the passionate love that overflows the prescribed limits to become violent. Like a spirited river whose waters break their bed, this feeling no longer contains itself, no longer listens to reason, no longer respects the customs. It is the love of Zulaykhā, the wife of Potiphar, for Joseph, her young servant — the precise Quranic example of out-of-the-norm feeling. The Quran uses it thus: “She became enamoured of him — qad shaghafahā ḥubban — she shut the doors of the palace and said to him: Here I am, yours!” (XII, 23). To make someone mad with love to the point of seeing them drown — that is indeed ash-shaghaf. Before this violent love, feminine modesty and social restraint are abolished. But the word, according to Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (1272-1350), keeps a proper sense — the envelope of the heart, that which girds the most vulnerable flesh. Shaghafahā ḥubban does not mean “he filled her with love” but, more exactly, “he reached the very envelope of her heart.” Not to be confused with shaʿaf, of a lesser intensity.

temoin.fr: He has touched even the envelope of her heart

chaghaf.meditation: What love, within you, has touched the most intimate envelope — the one you were protecting without knowing it?
chaghaf.ouverture: The Sufis read the episode of Zulaykhā as a veil placed over their own history with God. Rūmī defends her explicitly: when one knows Joseph, one ceases to blame Zulaykhā. To love too much, says the way, is not a flaw; to love falsely, yes. And the only way to know that one truly loves is to see whether love, in us, breaks its bed.

houbb-saiq.definition: Thunderstruck love, the lightning-bolt of love
houbb-saiq.description: Al-ḥubb aṣ-ṣāʿiq is, literally, thunderstruck, violent love, in a whirlwind. It is the precise Arabic equivalent of what English calls love at first sight — but with, in the root ṣ-ʿ-q, something more apocalyptic: the divine lightning, the one that strikes Mount Sinai in the Quran, the one that strikes down the soul and leaves it for dead before it rises transfigured. One also speaks of ṣaʿqatu al-ḥubb — the lightning of love — to denote that unique instant when, with no preparation whatever, the soul tips over. Before: ordinary life. After: another world, from which one will never again come out. The word says two things at once: the suddenness (the lightning does not announce itself) and the sacred violence (the lightning comes from heaven, and the one it strikes no longer belongs quite to himself). Hence its kinship with the mystical experience of ṣaʿaq — the ecstatic swoon of the one who has glimpsed the Presence and cannot bear it.

temoin.fr: And Moses fell thunderstruck

houbb-saiq.meditation: What lightning have you received — and from whom have you never come back?
houbb-saiq.ouverture: The Sufi way distinguishes the human ṣaʿaq — the lightning of love — from the divine ṣaʿaq, the theophanic lightning that strikes Moses on the mount. But the two are kin: they name the instant when the ego no longer suffices, when one must fall in order to be able to rise again other. Love at first sight, says Ibn ʿArabī, is a white death — the first.

houraq.definition: The burning, the blaze of love
houraq.description: Ḥurāq names the pure burning — the one that does not stop. One also speaks of nīrān al-ḥubb, the fires of love, an expression become a topos of the whole of Arabic poetry. This love, says the tradition, partakes of the brazier of the left hand that the lover feeds with a firebrand held in the right hand: it is he himself who keeps up the fire that consumes him, and he cannot help it. Al-ḥarīq derives from the same word and denotes a matter in combustion. Muḥriq is what burns, but also what purifies — for fire, in Arabic as in many traditions, is the ambiguous agent par excellence: it destroys, but it is in it that one bakes the bread and forges the sword. The link with mysticism is immediate: the Sufi way often speaks of the ḥaraq al-rūḥ — the combustion of the spirit — as the very process of spiritual purification. To burn with desire for God is not to be far from Him: it is to be already in His workshop.

temoin.fr: Burn me with Your fire, O Beloved

houraq.meditation: What fire do you feed with one hand, while it burns you with the other?
houraq.ouverture: For Ḥallāj, who ended literally burned on the pyre of Baghdad in 922, the mystical ḥurāq is not a metaphor: it is the very experience of union. You called me, he says to God, and I came. You willed me, and I became You. The fire that consumes the lover is also the one that reveals that there had never been two.

lada.description: Ladhʿ names a very particular quality of burning love: the climbing flame that surrounds a piece of wood or a branch, and that consumes them in a sonorous crackling of fibres and dry leaves. It is not a silent brazier, it is not an ember. It is the fire that sings as it burns — that makes noise as it eats what it loves. The word has a very close kinship with ladhgh, love-bite — the one that leaves no respite at any moment. The two are almost homophones, and the Arabic tradition gladly plays on this near-identity: to love thus is to burn and to be bitten at once. Classical poetry loves this word precisely because it makes heard what it says. When one pronounces ladhʿ, one already hears the crackling of the fibres in the flame.

temoin.fr: This ladhʿ gnaws like the flame

lada.meditation: What fire, within you, burns while making noise — and what does it say as it consumes?
lada.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish the silent fires and the sonorous fires: the first is that of the advanced soul, which burns without saying anything; the second, that of the soul young on the way, which still cries out. Ladhʿ belongs to this second category — it is not bad, it is young. Spiritual maturity consists in letting the crackling subside, without extinguishing the fire itself.

awar.description: Uwār al-ḥubb names the amorous flame in its incendiary character — love as a demanding and passionate prince, who suffers neither lukewarmness nor half-measure. For the Arabs, a love worthy of the name must not be lukewarm, in half-tints, without madness. The Arabic language very gladly grants it the title of emir — the one who commands without sharing. This emir, says the tradition, is made of precious matter: ebony wood and all sorts of fragrances. As if the blaze of love, in consuming the lover, also released a perfume — like the incense that yields its scent only by letting itself be burned. The uwār is not only destruction: it is the liberation of what was imprisoned in matter. The word belongs to the high poetic register. One meets it among the great Arab elegists — from the pre-Islamic muʿallaqāt to the Abbasid dīwāns — as an almost ritual formula to say love-fire.

temoin.fr: She cast her hands into love, and kindled its fire

awar.meditation: What perfume, within you, is released only by the flame?
awar.ouverture: Ibn ʿArabī taught that the human heart is capable of all forms; it is also capable, says the tradition, of all temperatures. The lukewarm is not a station, it is an absence. True love chooses its extreme — the blaze above, silence below. Between the two, there is nothing one could truly call loving.

gharam.definition: The love that makes one weep, sentimental love
gharam.description: Gharām is the love that makes one weep like the sea, sentimental love such as one no longer finds except in old love songs. But the etymology conceals something harder: the root gh-r-m also means debt, fine, obligation. Gharām is therefore love as a debt that one can no longer repay — the love that holds us because we owe it something we cannot give back. In popular poetry, in song, in the epics, the term recurs almost as much as ḥubb. Egyptian song in particular has developed this notion of gharām, to the point of making of it a genre in its own right. Umm Kulthūm, Abdel-Halim Hafez, Farīd al-Aṭrash made of it the material of their most heart-rending lamentations — and the word maghrūm (“I am in love”) recurs there like a refrain. The Quran uses the word in the sense of chastisement, of lasting punishment (XXV, 65). This ambiguity is precious: gharām says, in a single sound, love and its punishment, the feeling and its price.

temoin.fr: Its chastisement is, truly, perpetual

gharam.meditation: What debt of love do you carry — and what do you pay, without being able to settle it?
gharam.ouverture: The Sufis have magnificently turned this word over: what the Quran names perpetual chastisement becomes, in their reading, love that does not end. The perpetuity of gharām is terrible for the one who refuses it, but glorious for the one who accepts it. Ḥallāj, on the pyre, would not have repaid his debt for all the gold in the world — he loved it.

junun.description: Junūn is the Arabic word for madness — but its etymology conceals a profound intuition. The root j-n-n means to cover, to veil, to hide. From it come also janna (the garden, a protected place) and jinn (the invisible beings that people the other world). The madman is therefore not, in Arabic, simply the one who has lost reason: he is the one whom a veil has covered, whom another world henceforth inhabits. In the amorous lexicon, junūn names the very principle on which Majnūn — Qays ibn al-Mulawwaḥ — loved Laylā unto death. Majnūn literally means the one who has been covered, the one possessed by a jinn — but the tradition at once turns the word over: if one is possessed by love, which is the envoy of the Real, then madness is the highest of lucidities. The boundary between happy love and unhappy love is, in junūn, infinitely narrow: before being erotic, the Arabs are first sentimental, who attach to love the various attributes of their states of soul. Junūn is one of these attributes — the one that makes the one struck by it lose reason.

temoin.fr:
Have you not seen how love draws its own to itself,
as one draws the madman by the arm of his chain?

junun.meditation: From what madness do you believe yourself protected — and what does this protection cover, within you?
junun.ouverture: The Sufi way takes up the whole literature of Majnūn to make of it a parable of the path: to enter into the Presence, one must accept that a veil be laid over ordinary reason. To become mad in God is, for the uqalāʾ al-majānīn (the “sane madmen” of Islam), the station in which one ceases to be a prisoner of one's own judgement. The madness of love is, sometimes, the only reason left intact.

houyam.description: Huyām is love-perdition, the one that makes of the lover a wandering dervish — in the primary, etymological sense: the fact of driving someone mad. The delight of this feeling is such that the lover can exile himself to the other end of the world, lose the sense of realities, forget who he was. The word comes from the technical vocabulary that the Bedouins reserved for the camelids. Hayman was said of a camel struck by a terrible thirst, which wanders in the desert unable to quench itself, and which nothing calms. Majnūn, the most famous lover of the tradition, made it his own: “Like the camel struck with huyām, no water quenches my thirst, and my thirst is slow to make me perish.” There is here a decisive image: the lover is not only thirsty — he is thirsty with a thirst that nothing slakes. To drink would not help. Nor would to die, for death would be too quick. Huyām is the station in which one learns that certain thirsts are their own answer.

temoin.fr: Like the huyām of the camel that no watering-place quenches

houyam.meditation: What thirst do you carry, that you now know to be unquenchable?
houyam.ouverture: For the Sufis, the huyām is precisely the station in which one understands that the soul was not created to be quenched by the creatures. Saint Augustine said the same thing in Latin (Inquietum est cor nostrum). Thirst itself is the sign — the ontological proof — that there is something beyond, of which the thirst is the trace in hollow.

hiyam.definition: Distraught love, the heart in collapse
hiyam.description: Hiyām is the direct cousin of huyām, formed of the same root, and one says hāʾim al-qalb to signify a heart smitten with love, obsessed, in collapse. The nuance is subtle: huyām insists on the wandering, hiyām on the obsession — the state of the heart that can no longer think of anything else, that turns around the beloved as the dervish around his axis. The same root yields hāmā, yahīmu — to lose the north, to ramble, to wander aimlessly. The hāʾim lover no longer has a direction: what the pilgrims seek by the qibla, he has lost. But this loss is not a lack: it is, for the poets, the sign that one has entered the dimension of love in which the ordinary directions no longer count. The Quran uses the root in a contemptuous sense — to describe the poets: “Do you not see that in every valley they wander (yahīmūn)?” (XXVI, 225). But the Sufis have turned the verse over: yes, says Ibn ʿArabī, the true poets wander in every valley, because one must traverse all the valleys of love to arrive at the One who hollowed them out.

temoin.fr: Do you not see that, in every valley, they wander?

hiyam.meditation: In which valley do you wander, no longer knowing whether you are lost there or at last at home?
hiyam.ouverture: ʿAṭṭār, in his Conference of the Birds, makes his birds traverse precisely seven valleys — quest, love, knowledge, independence, oneness, stupefaction, annihilation. The hiyām belongs to the sixth: stupor, there where one no longer knows anything, and where this not-knowing is exactly the knowledge that remained to be acquired.

mustaham.definition: The one whom love has made distraught
mustaham.description: Mustahām and istahāma are other declensions of the words huyām and hiyām — always the same root h-y-m, always the same image: the thirsty soul of the camel in the desert. The prefix musta- adds a precious nuance: it is no longer only the state, it is the person entirely defined by this state. Al-mustahām is the one who has become the huyām, who no longer has any other identity. In mysticism, haymān is said of the psychological state, and muhayman of the being subjected to mad love. And here is an extraordinary reversal of the language: Al-Muhaymin — formed on the same root — is one of the 99 Names of Allāh, usually translated as “the Watchful, the Preserver.” A Sufi etymology reads in it also: the One who makes souls distraught, the God whose love makes those who approach Him lose reason. Thus, what denotes the mad lover also denotes God Himself. The language itself indicates that love, at its highest, no longer distinguishes the lover from his Beloved.

temoin.fr: He is God, beside whom there is no divinity — the Muhaymin

mustaham.meditation: What, within you, has made you so distraught that it has become your identity?
mustaham.ouverture: When a word of the human lexicon and a divine Name share the same root, says Ibn ʿArabī, it is a sign: the language itself has perceived that there was something to understand. Mustahām and Muhaymin are not confused — but they illumine each other. To love unto losing oneself is to meet within oneself the Name that was losing you.

walah.definition: Amorous madness, the intoxication of absence
walah.description: Walah says love-madness in a particular way: it is the state of the one who loses his mind and who becomes drunk on the absence of the beloved. Love is still there — but it is love inhabited by lack, the love that lives only on what is lacking. The derivatives of the root are expressive: walihūn (in the plural) are the lovers become mad with absence; tawallaha is the verb that says to stray from oneself; walhāne qualifies the state of amorous straying; muwallah is the one who is consumed by grief, gone astray. The lexicographical tradition specifies the degrees: walah is a state less ravaging than khabl (pure dementia) but stronger than kalāf (demanding love). It is, in a way, the middle madness — the one in which one is still aware of being mad, which only aggravates the torment.

temoin.fr: The heart has gone astray, since you

walah.meditation: Of what are you drunk, that is no longer there?
walah.ouverture: The Sufis have seen in walah the exact analogue of the mystical desire for God in His apparent absence: one knows Him to be there, one feels Him absent, and it is this absence itself that becomes the nourishment of love. “His nearness is His distance, says an anonymous adage, and His distance is His nearness.” The walah is the state in which one accepts this paradox.

tadlih.definition: The love that turns life upside down
tadlih.description: Tadlīh names love-madness from a very precise angle: the one in which love seizes the life of the lover and turns it upside down. Before, one had projects, habits, a schedule, a sketched-out future. And then love comes — and all that one believed founded reveals itself suspended, negotiable, with no more weight. The root d-l-h means precisely to overturn, to shake, to make one lose one's head. The mudallah is the one who no longer knows where he lives, who forgets his way, who no longer recognises his face in the mirror. But unlike khabl, which is radical dementia, tadlīh keeps something juvenile, almost spring-like — it is the madness that comes from the excess of life, not from lack. The word appears often in adab prose to describe the states of the very young lovers — those whom love discovers alive for the first time.

temoin.fr: Love overturned him, and he went out of himself

tadlih.meditation: What, in your life, awaits being turned upside down?
tadlih.ouverture: The Sufi way distinguishes the fatal overturnings and the fruitful overturnings. The tadlīh is, by nature, fruitful: something in it wants life, even at the price of disorder. It is, says Rūmī, the gentle revolution — the one that does not destroy in order to destroy, but that undoes in order to make better.

khabl.definition: The dementia of having lost the object of love
khabl.description: Khabl names the loss of all bearings in the one who suffers in his flesh and in his soul. It is pure dementia — radical dementia — of the lover who has lost the object of his love. The madman, al-majnūn, is also called al-mukhabbal. And the tradition makes no great difference between the madness that comes from the jinn (junūn) and the one that comes from lost love (khabl): in both cases, the soul has entered a world from which it does not come back as it came into it. Unlike tadlīh, which can be joyful, khabl is always grave. The mukhabbal lover is not in the fruitful disorder of young love; he is in collapse, in disaster. He no longer speaks to the living; when he speaks, he speaks to the dead. When he walks, he no longer knows where he is going. When he eats, he no longer feels the taste. It is the state that Qays knew, says the tradition, after the definitive estrangement of Laylā. Later, it is the state into which Rūmī would be plunged after the disappearance of Shams. But it is also from this state that Rūmī would draw, on coming out, the six volumes of the Mathnawī.

temoin.fr: Love has dismantled me: I am no longer I

khabl.meditation: What have you lost that has driven you mad — and what have you built, since, from the dismantling?
khabl.ouverture: The khabl is, for the Sufis, the antechamber of the fanāʾ — mystical annihilation. Not every disintegration is a death: some are the paring-down necessary so that something else may come to be. Rūmī, after Shams, no longer wrote anything as before. The dismantling had made room for the Mathnawī.

iktiyab.description: Iktiyāb is love-sorrow, melancholy in the ancient medical sense — the one that colours the face with black, that slows the blood, that veils the eyes. Muktaʾiba is said, in classical Arabic, of an ash that turns black; by analogy, the face of the muktaʾib lover is sombre, as if darkened from within — not by smoke but by the pain he feels at the loss of his love, or at the impossibility of rejoining it. The Persian language knows the same state: dard, gham — pain, grief. Arabic also has its expression: imtiḥān al-ḥubb, the trial of love. As if true love comprised, by nature, a part of trial without which it would not reveal itself for what it is. The Arabic tradition does not flee melancholy: it posits it as one of the legitimate stations of love, sometimes necessary to the maturation of the feeling. Constant gaiety would be suspect; the pain traversed, on the contrary, is a proof.

temoin.fr: True love is the one whose soul is melancholy

iktiyab.meditation: What pain, within you, proves a love that nothing else could attest?
iktiyab.ouverture: The ancient Arab physicians — al-Rāzī, Ibn Sīnā — described the melancholy of love as a true illness, with its course, its symptoms, its prognosis. But they added that its cure did not depend on the physician: it depended on the return of the beloved, or on a higher grace. The Sufis understood: the higher grace is always there, ready. One had only to have been ill in the right place to recognise it.

houzn.definition: Sadness, the grave state of love
houzn.description: Ḥuzn means sadness. The word is used — improperly, say the purists — to denote love, whereas it applies perfectly to one of its states, notably in the shajw or the lahf. But this slipped usage is not an impoverishment: it says something precise about Arabic love, which considers that gravity is one of the constitutive qualities of true feeling. The root ḥ-z-n has a biblical reach: Jacob, in the Quran, is the archetype of the ḥazīn — the one who weeps until he loses his sight, because of the disappearance of Joseph (XII, 84). The sadness of love is therefore not a weakness, in Arabic culture: it is a prophetic posture, the sign that the heart has not forgotten. Muhammad himself, says the tradition, lived a ʿām al-ḥuzn — a year of sadness — after the death of his wife Khadīja and of his uncle Abū Ṭālib. The word entered Islamic historiography without anyone ever seeking to attenuate it: sadness is part of the path.

temoin.fr: And his eyes whitened with sadness

houzn.meditation: What sadness within you attests that your heart has not forgotten?
houzn.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish two sadnesses: the one that dries up — one must beware of it; and the one that hollows out, that makes the heart deeper, more capable of receiving. The second is a maqām, a station of the path. Rūmī said: “The wound is the place by which the light enters.”

kamad.description: Kamad names a very precise state: an interiorised and repressed pain, whose action is to make the lover livid — to give him a colour that is not his own, to disturb him so much that his face, formerly limpid, turns blue, transforms. Where ḥuzn is seen in tears, where iktiʾāb is seen in the general darkening, kamad, for its part, is seen only in the pallor of the skin. It is the pain of the one who does not complain, who does not weep, who says nothing — and who, nonetheless, is dying slowly inside. The state is also called al-kamāʾ. It is one of the most common, paradoxically, in the Arabic conception of love: for modesty often prevents one from speaking; but what one does not say, the body manifests. The face that decomposes is the involuntary confession of what the mouth refuses to formulate.

temoin.fr: The pain fell silent, and the face took on its colour

kamad.meditation: What pain, within you, does not allow itself to speak — but leaves you, in the mirror, unrecognisable?
kamad.ouverture: The Sufis work precisely on this mute part: what one keeps silent, one carries twice. The practice of dhikr is not only the remembrance of God — it is also the loosening of the tongue, the permission to say what one no longer dared. To love heals, provided one accepts to name the pain one has repressed.

lahf.definition: Sadness-reminiscence, the obsession with what haunts
lahf.description: Lahf is one of the painful states of love, marked by sadness and reminiscence. Sometimes it is the obsession with what haunts, what inhabits and consumes — the thought that returns ceaselessly to the vanished beloved, to the lost gesture, to the word unsaid. The root l-h-f evokes the impulse of regret, the sigh that escapes without one's having decided it. Talahhafa is the verb that says to sigh with regret, to lament over what one has lost. But lahf is not only nostalgia: it is active nostalgia, the one that consumes in the present. The Arabic tradition has long noted that lahf is cousin to ḥuzn, but with this nuance: lahf insists on the whirling of thought. It is the love that returns in a loop. It is the mind that cannot extinguish itself, because a single name keeps it awake.

temoin.fr: O regret of my soul, over days gone by

lahf.meditation: What name returns within you, like a beat that nothing stops?
lahf.ouverture: The Sufi practice of dhikr turns the lahf over: if a thought must inhabit ceaselessly, then let it be the divine Name — not as a distraction from the pain, but as its recapitulation. What you name obsessively, says the way, tells you who you are.

lam.description: Al-lam is one of the variants of love-unreason, since it is a love that haunts the thought of the one who feels it — al-lamām. The beloved being is said to be alimu of the one who loves him, that is, very near to his heart and to his thoughts, to the point of invading the whole mental space. The root l-m-m is rich: al-lamāma also means, in the Quran, the light faults, the trifles (sūra LIII, 32). A precious ambiguity: what haunts the thought — and seems so grave to us — sometimes amounts, seen from elsewhere, to trifles. The lover does not know how to measure rightly: the slightest absence becomes a drama, the slightest gesture a treasure. The word also says something of contiguity: the beloved is glued to the thoughts of the one who loves him, as one says of a thing that it is limāmiyya, contiguous. To love thus is to be no longer able to separate one's own thoughts from the silhouette of the other.

temoin.fr: Save the light faults

lam.meditation: What clings to your thoughts, to the point of preventing you from thinking alone?
lam.ouverture: The Sufis teach that there exist two modes of presence to thoughts: the glued presence (al-lam) that obsesses, and the traversing presence that illumines. The way consists in passing from the first to the second — not by driving away the beloved presence, but by letting it be what it is: a trace of the unique Presence.

istihtar.definition: Distraught love, the ravage of care
istihtar.description: In the mystical vocabulary of al-Daylamī (10th century), a great Sufi theoretician of love, istihtār refers to a very precise state of distraught love: the one in which “unreason and remembrance multiply, and in which the excess of care and of stupor turns you away from every being other than the Beloved.” The root h-t-r is interesting: it evokes the fact of exhausting, of uttering ceaselessly. The mustahtir lover is the one who no longer has enough words to say what he lives, and who repeats them to no end — as the dervish repeats the Name until the Name repeats him. It is a very high state in the Sufi classification of love: it is the state in which the lover can no longer think of anything that is not the Beloved. Every other attention has become impossible. The world is reduced to a single name — and this name, henceforth, occupies all the room.

temoin.fr:
Vigil and remembrance have multiplied within me —
care and stupor have turned me away from every other

istihtar.meditation: What memory has so multiplied within you that it leaves you nothing else to see?
istihtar.ouverture: The istihtār is, in Daylamī's lexicon, the immediate threshold before the fanāʾ — mystical annihilation. Before disappearing entirely into the Beloved, the soul passes through this state in which the Beloved already occupies all the room. The passage from one to the other is now but an infinitesimal movement: it suffices to cease knowing oneself as distinct.

souhd.description: Suhd names the insomnia of love — that particular category of nocturnal wakefulness which is neither anxiety, nor work, nor chosen meditation. It is the heart that prevents the eyelids from closing because it is on the watch. For what? An absence, a sign, the possible return — or simply the night itself, because it is the only moment when one can be alone with one's pain. The Hijazi poet al-ʿArjī (694-740) left this verse that the whole tradition has kept: “I do not taste sleep until dawn — which I watch for as the guard keeping vigil over the breach.” The word says this watch, this armed vigil that the insomnia of love is. In Persian, a heart afflicted and careworn by love is said to be del-dard-mand — heart bearing pain. The night it traverses does not have the same weight as the other nights.

temoin.fr:
I do not taste sleep until dawn —
I watch for it as the guard keeping vigil over the breach

souhd.meditation: What vigil do you keep, that no day relieves?
souhd.ouverture: The suhd is also the mystical name of the spiritual vigil. The Sufi too keeps his vigil — not for a human love but for a Beloved who may arrive at any hour. The kinship is not accidental: the insomniac lover and the awakened mystic practise the same attention, simply directed differently. And sometimes, in the same night, the one becomes the other.

wasl.description: Waṣl names union properly speaking — the moment when the two lovers rejoin each other at last, when the separation ceases. But the word also carries something denser: it says the powerful bond, the joyful reunion, the romantic fusion. The whole experience of the amorous meeting — from the instant of the crossing of gazes to the marriage of souls — can be named by this single word. The root w-ṣ-l is one of the most generous in Arabic: it yields the verb waṣala (to link, to join, to reach), the noun ṣilat al-raḥim (the maintaining of family bonds, an essential religious duty), the grammatical concept of mawṣūl (the relative pronoun that links two clauses). To love is to perform the same operation as the one by which grammar itself unites its sentences: to link what was separated. In mysticism, al-waṣl is the very name of the ultimate station of the spiritual path: union with God. It is the exact opposite of hijr, separation. The whole Sufi journey can be described as a movement between these two poles: hijr and waṣl, exile and return.

temoin.fr: Whoever has tasted union has tasted life

wasl.meditation: With what, or with whom, are you linked — to the point that your life depends on it?
wasl.ouverture: For Ibn ʿArabī, the waṣl is not a conquest but a recognition. We were never separated from the Real; we were separated from the awareness of not being so. The waṣl does not create a new bond: it reveals to us a bond that was there forever, covered by the veil of our inattention.

habl.description: Ḥabl first names the rope in the concrete sense — the one that one knots, the one that holds, the one that one undoes only with great difficulty. But the word was very early taken up in a metaphorical sense to denote the amorous bond: the fetter, the ring, the indissoluble bond that unites two beings to the point that one can no longer separate them without breaking what holds them to themselves. In Algeria, one says taḥabbala to signify to bind oneself for life. And the Arab matrimonial tradition gladly speaks of the ḥabl of marriage — the rope that is not a chain, because it has been chosen, and because it is the very instrument of security. The notion gains in abstraction in the waṣl: the latter keeps the connotation of dynamic union, whereas ḥabl insists on the attachment, the materiality of the bond. One rejoins someone through the waṣl; one remains bound to him through the ḥabl.

temoin.fr: Hold fast, all of you, to the rope of God

habl.meditation: What bond, within you, has become impossible to undo — and you no longer wish to undo?
habl.ouverture: The Quranic verse transposes the amorous ḥabl to the divine plane: the rope of God to which the believer holds fast is precisely that ḥabl — not an external obligation, but the intimate bond to which one holds because one has felt that it holds us. To love God is to cease wanting to undo what was holding us upright.

khoulla.description: Khulla derives from takhallala, which means amorous interpenetration — the interpenetration of two beings to the point that one can no longer separate what is the one from what is the other. It is love and friendship intermingled, in a measure so perfect that no English word renders it precisely. The word is in use above all in the poetico-mystical universe. It is a love-fusion with God whose model is al-Khalīl — the Arabic surname of the patriarch Abraham, the Friend par excellence, the one whose love has entirely fused with its object. The Quran says it explicitly: “God took Abraham for an intimate friend” (IV, 125). The khulla is therefore, in the mystical hierarchy, superior even to ḥubb and to maḥabba: it is friendship-fusion, the state in which the lover no longer loves the Beloved — he is His friend to the point that the contours of each have been effaced. The khulla, says the tradition, is not given to all: it is the grace reserved for the khulafāʾ, the elect of love.

temoin.fr: And God took Abraham for an intimate friend

khoulla.meditation: What interpenetration have you lived, in which you no longer knew what of you came from you?
khoulla.ouverture: The Sufis say that there are two modes of divine nearness: maḥabba, in which love makes God present, and khulla, in which the presence is so total that there is no longer any need to make it present. The first mode is that of Moses (al-Kalīm, the one to whom one speaks); the second is that of Abraham (al-Khalīl, the intimate friend). All the Abrahamic spirituality of Islam, Judaism and Christianity is held in this word.

khilm.definition: Friendship-tenderness, the good affection of companions
khilm.description: Khilm derives from mukhālama, which means the good affection of two companions, the friendly tenderness, their mutual longanimity. A friend is called al-khilmu; the gathered companions, al-akhlām. Where khulla says fusion, khilm says tender fidelity. It is no longer the incandescence of ʿishq, nor the ecstasy of waṣl — it is a more discreet quality: love become a lasting companionship, the love that no longer burns but still warms, for a long time. The Arabic tradition has placed under this word the ideal of marital friendship — the one that survives passion and nourishes it with patience. Like ilf, the khilm belongs to the family of the soothed loves, but with this nuance: ilf is the art of living beside, khilm is the art of living with. The nuance is minute but essential.

temoin.fr: Your khilm is the one who tells you the truth in joy as in pain

khilm.meditation: With whom have you ceased to play a role — and who, in return, no longer plays his own?
khilm.ouverture: The khilm has, in the Sufi way, its equivalent: the ṣuḥba, the spiritual companionship between the master and the disciple, or between two disciples committed to the same way. To love someone — or God — thus is to cease seeking the radiance, and to find the stable presence that no longer burns but that remains.

istikana.definition: Soothing, the consent to submit to the beloved
istikana.description: Istikāna names the desire to be conjoined with the beloved, in soothing oneself at his contact and in accepting to submit oneself to him. It is not the submission of the slave before the master — it is the consented relaxing of the one who has so awaited this meeting that he yields at the moment it arrives, when emotion makes the heart and the legs go soft. The root k-w-n is the root of being itself (kāna, to be); and sakana means to rest, to dwell in peace. The istikāna is therefore that very particular state in which one becomes at last by ceasing to resist — in which one is by settling. As in the Quranic verse: “It is He who made the sakīna descend into the hearts of the believers.” There also exists a near word, saqīma, which evokes the physical suffering of the lover. The boundary is narrow: the soothing of the heart in union and the exhaustion of the body after the waiting are, in Arabic, immediate cousins.

temoin.fr: It is He who made the sakīna descend into the hearts of the believers

istikana.meditation: Before what, within you, has the combat at last given way — not through defeat, but through soothing?
istikana.ouverture: The Sufis see in the sakīna — the etymological sister of istikāna — the very quality of the soul that has arrived at rest in God. It is not the end of the path: it is the path become rest. To love thus is to cease combating the beloved, to cease combating oneself — and to find, in this twofold abandon, the peace one was seeking through effort.

alaqa.description: ʿAlāqa names the irresistible attraction of the beloved, particularly at the moment of the conquest. But the word, for the poet, goes further: there is no conquest that does not leave its indelible trace. The attachment that results from it is one of the arcana of love. The root ʿ-l-q is expressive: it means to cling, to hang, to remain attached to. It is the same root that yields ʿalaq, the clot of blood or the hanging thing of which the Quran speaks concerning the creation of the embryo (XCVI, 2). The amorous ʿalāqa is therefore, etymologically, what attaches itself to the heart as the embryo attaches itself to the uterine wall: by vital adherence. Taʿallaqa is the verb that says this attachment, iltaṣaqa reinforces still further the idea of indissociable adherence. The word has, in modern Arabic, kept all its nuances: one speaks of an ʿalāqa between two people to denote a relationship in the strong sense — something that lasts, that clings, that marks.

temoin.fr: He created the human being from an adherence

alaqa.meditation: What trace have you received, that does not efface itself — and that you no longer wish to see effaced?
alaqa.ouverture: The kinship between the amorous ʿalāqa and the Quranic ʿalaq (the clot from which the human comes) is, for the Sufis, highly significant. Creation itself is made by adherence; human love is, at another level, its faithful repetition. Every amorous conquest remakes, in miniature, the creation of the world.

moulaaba.definition: Amorous teasing, the play of complicity
moulaaba.description: Mulāʿaba names those amorous teasings which, often, lead to istimtāʿ — the pleasure linked to amorous company, the contentment of the senses, voluptuousness. It is the play of lovers who know each other enough to be able to tease one another without wounding one another, to bicker without losing one another, to vex one another knowing that it will end in laughter. A laughing woman, in the tradition, is said to be laʿūb — the playful, the player. The word has the same root as laʿiba, to play, and as luʿba, the game itself. To love in the mulāʿaba is to play together — not as one plays a game one seeks to win, but as one plays an instrument one loves: for the pleasure of the sound. The station of the reunion would be incomplete without this word. For union is not only the grave fusion (khulla), nor the stable bond (ḥabl) — it is also that regained lightness which makes it so that two beings, after all the journey, can still laugh together.

temoin.fr: The best of you is the one who is best to his own

moulaaba.meditation: With whom can you still laugh without watchfulness, without calculation, without a shield?
moulaaba.ouverture: The Sufi way does not forget laughter. The Prophet laughed with his wives, joked with his companions, played with his grandchildren. The grave spirituality that excludes play, says the tradition, is not the spirituality of the Prophet. To love God is also to be able to laugh in His presence — as one laughs with a friend one no longer needs to impress.

houbb-ilahi.definition: Divine love, the reciprocity of Creator and creature
houbb-ilahi.description: Al-ḥubb al-ilāhī is divine love in the technical sense: the love that passes between God and His creature, in both directions. It is entirely founded upon the reciprocity stated by one of the Quranic verses most meditated upon in the mystical tradition: “He loves them, and they love Him” — yuḥibbuhum wa yuḥibbūnahu (V, 54). The miracle of this verse, says Rūmī, lies in the order of the words. It is not the creature that loves first and God who responds. It is God who loves first, and the creature does no more than answer this prior call. All human love for God is, in reality, the echo of a divine love already cast forth. “You would not have sought Me,” said Pascal, “had you not already found Me” — and the thought is exactly Quranic. Al-ḥubb al-ilāhī is the technical equivalent of the wadd and the wudd of which we have spoken: it is the same root, but carried to its maximal intensity. All the paths of the amorous lexicon lead back to this word — as all the rivers lead back to the sea.

temoin.fr: He loves them, and they love Him

houbb-ilahi.meditation: What, within you, is first the answer to a call — and not the initiative?
houbb-ilahi.ouverture: The whole Sufi way is held in this reversal: we are not the lovers of God; we are the beloved who respond. To love Him amounts to consenting to a love that preceded us. This is why, says Ibn ʿArabī, one does not conquer God: one lets Him be what He already was within us.

houbb-al-houbb.definition: The love of love, love at its purest power
houbb-al-houbb.description: Ḥubb al-ḥubb — the love of love — is an expression borrowed from Muslim mysticism and particularly dear to Ibn ʿArabī (1165-1240), the Sheikh al-Akbar, the greatest master. The concept says this: there exists a degree of love where one no longer loves this or that particular object — one loves love itself, as principle, as energy, as a name of God. Ibn ʿArabī inscribed it in this quatrain that has become emblematic: When I knew that love was beyond price, yet that I had no hold upon it until the term of my life, I fell forever in love with the love of love! It is, in the Sufi hierarchy, an advanced station: one has loved such a face, such a voice, such a beauty; and then, in a transformative intuition, one understands that all these objects were only windows opened upon one same reality — Love itself. From that point on, one no longer falls in love with a being: one is in love with the capacity to love, which passes through all beings without stopping at any.

temoin.fr: I have fallen in love with the love of love

houbb-al-houbb.meditation: Have you ever loved the loving more than what you loved?
houbb-al-houbb.ouverture: This formula is, for the way of Ibn ʿArabī, the antechamber of the tawḥīd of love: to recognise that all loves point toward one same Love, which has no limited object because it is the Origin of every object. It is also the experience that Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya described, but formulated philosophically by Ibn ʿArabī: to love God is to love that by which all loves are possible.

houbb-al-llah.definition: The love of the pious person for God
houbb-al-llah.description: Ḥubb Allāh is the love of the pious person for Allah — the active version of divine love, the one in which the servant takes the initiative of answering the call he has recognised. It is the love-cause-of-piety and the love-effect-of-piety both together. A famous ḥadīth of the Prophet indicates the way: “Withdraw from this world, and Allah will love you; detach yourself from what people possess, and people will love you.” The piety described is not a flight — it is a detachment. Divine love is not refused to the one who also loves the world, but it reveals itself fully only to the one who asks nothing further of it, who loves the world in God and not against Him. It is also the love that Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya — the great woman saint of Basra in the eighth century — definitively reformulated against mercantile motives. If I love You out of fear of hell, burn me in hell. If I love You in hope of paradise, exclude me from it. But if I love You for Yourself, do not deprive me of Your beauty. With her, ḥubb Allāh becomes pure love — without calculation, without counterpart.

temoin.fr: Withdraw from this world, and Allah will love you

houbb-al-llah.meditation: For what do you love — truly? For the pain avoided? For the reward hoped for? Or for that which loves, within itself?
houbb-al-llah.ouverture: The love of Rābiʿa — to love God for God, without self-interest — displaced the spirituality of Islam as a whole. Before her, one spoke of obedience. After her, one could speak of love without blushing. It is one of the silent revolutions of Sufism, and it is the work of a woman.

wadoud.definition: The Loving — the divine aptitude to love without counting
wadoud.description: Wadūd is the aptitude to love without counting. The word is, above all, one of the 99 Most Beautiful Names of God — al-Asmāʾ al-Ḥusnā. The Quran uses it twice: “My Lord is Most Merciful, Most Loving” (XI, 90) and “He is the All-Forgiving, the Loving” (LXXXV, 14). The Name says this: God is Wadūd in His very being — not only by the effect of a decision, not at times, not toward some. He is Loving. It is His intrinsic attribute. And since all that exists proceeds from Him, the ontological conclusion imposes itself: love is not a thing that exists in the world — it is that by which the world exists. The tradition also sees in wadūd a trait of the Arab personality itself — as if the language had recognised, in the divine Loving One, the archetype of a quality it wished to cultivate in the human. To love like al-Wadūd is to love in superabundance, without keeping the accounts.

temoin.fr: And He is the All-Forgiving, the Loving

wadoud.meditation: How do you love — by measuring, or in superabundance?
wadoud.ouverture: Ibn ʿArabī teaches that the divine Names are not external qualifiers attributed to God — they are the modes of action of the Real in the world. When the human loves without counting, it is not he who loves: it is al-Wadūd who acts through him. To love, at this level, no longer separates the human from the divine — it binds them to the point of identification.

tatayoum.definition: Love-adoration, the enthralment to the beloved
tatayoum.description: Tatayyum names love-adoration — the state in which the lover no longer possesses himself, in which he is enthralled to the point that everything he does, he does in reference to the beloved. The root t-y-m yields taymū Allāh, an expression that means “enthralled to God,” “who adores God.” It is exactly the sense of the proper name Taym Allāh, frequent in Arabic onomastics. The kinship of tatayyum with al-yatm — to become an orphan — is no accident: the root is the same. The mutayyam lover is in the position of the orphan: he has lost what gave him his own place, and henceforth he depends on the beloved in order to exist. The word therefore carries a rich ambiguity: it says at once enthralment and devotion, servitude and adoration. In the Sufi lexicon, this ambiguity is cultivated: it is precisely when the human renounces his own sovereignty that he becomes free in the spiritual sense — free of what is not the Beloved.

temoin.fr: Your love has enthralled me: there is no flight possible now

tatayoum.meditation: To whom do you still believe you belong — once you have understood to whom you truly belong?
tatayoum.ouverture: The Sufi way radicalises this intuition: the free human is the one who has accepted to be the slave of God. All the other enthralments — to money, to fear, to the image of oneself — are servitudes. Only the enthralment to the Beloved is, paradoxically, liberation. The names ʿabd Allāh (“servant of God”), ʿabd al-Wadūd, Taymullāh all carry this inversion.

taabboud.description: Taʿabbud is love-adoration in the specifically religious context. The word applies to human relations in the strong sense — there is something of adoration in certain loves — but above all to the love of God. It derives from the same root as ʿabd (the servant), ʿibāda (faith, prayer, veneration) and maʿbūd (the adored one). The word therefore says precisely this: love for God is not a private emotion — it is a service freely consented to, a worship that structures the lover's whole life. Not a fluctuating feeling; a stable posture. Not intoxication alone, but intoxication organised into rite, into daily prayer, into fasting, into pilgrimage, into generosity to the poor, into remembrance. The Arabic language does not have the European fold that opposes love and duty: for it, duty is the form that love takes when it inscribes itself in time. Taʿabbud is this marriage of feeling and act — love as a manner of being every day.

temoin.fr: I created jinn and humankind only that they might worship Me

taabboud.meditation: What love inscribes itself, within you, in daily gestures — not through constraint, but because it must somehow be made visible?
taabboud.ouverture: For the Sufis, verse 56 of sura LI is read through a gloss attributed to Ibn ʿAbbās, the Prophet's cousin: “... that they might know Me.” To adore, in the spiritual language, is to recognise. And to recognise is to love. The trilogy service-knowledge-love is one in true taʿabbud.

fana.description: Fanāʾ is annihilation in the technical sense: the disappearance of individual consciousness into the consciousness of the Beloved. It is, in the Sufi hierarchy, the ultimate station or its immediate threshold. The lover no longer knows himself as distinct from the Beloved; the two are no longer but one — not by physical fusion, but by exhaustion of difference. The word covers two movements: either the wasting away (nuḥūl) and the evanescence of the lover (saqam) — love as the progressive erosion of the self —, or the elation and the mystico-religious intoxication (sukr) — love as submersion by the Presence. The two do not exclude one another; they are the two faces of the same movement. The fanāʾ is not a goal to be reached by effort. It is what happens when love has done its work — when all the other attachments have given way, and there remains only the Beloved. After the fanāʾ comes, in the tradition, the baqāʾ — the permanence in God: life that resumes, but transfigured.

temoin.fr:
Every being upon the earth is bound to vanish;
only the face of your Lord subsists

fana.meditation: What part of you has already passed — without your having mourned it?
fana.ouverture: It is upon this verse that the whole mysticism of fanāʾ takes root. If all passes save the Face, then to love truly is to love what does not pass — and to accept, in mirror, to let pass within oneself all that must pass. The fanāʾ is not a death: it is the purification. What dies is what had to die; what remains is what had to remain.

sukr.definition: Mystical intoxication, the vertigo of the Presence
sukr.description: Sukr is intoxication — in the literal sense first (the intoxication of wine), then transposed to the mystical sense. It is the term used in the mystico-religious domain to describe the distraught desire for God and the longing to meet Him, the state of the lover elated by the divine call. A controversy runs through the whole of Muslim mysticism: should one prefer sukr (ecstatic intoxication) or ṣaḥw (sobriety, awakened lucidity)? The disciples of Abū Yazīd al-Bisṭāmī (ninth century) chose sukr — intense intoxication, paradoxical sayings (shaṭaḥāt), radiance. Those of al-Junayd of Baghdad preferred ṣaḥw — measure, the return to consciousness, the mastery of effects. Both schools are true. The sukr is the moment of the meeting, where love makes one lose one's senses. The ṣaḥw is the moment of duration, where one must know how to return without losing anything of what one has tasted. The Sufi sage traverses, in his life, both states in alternation — like the wave that rises and that falls.

temoin.fr: Pour me the wine of Your love, O Beloved
temoin.source: Topos of Persian and Arabic mystical poetry

sukr.meditation: Of what wine are you intoxicated, that comes from no vineyard?
sukr.ouverture: The wine sung by Ḥāfiẓ, Rūmī, Ibn al-Fāriḍ — the mystical khamr — is not a metaphor of true wine: it is the reverse. Ordinary wine is itself an imperfect metaphor of spiritual intoxication. Divine love intoxicates in reality; all the rest only tries to imitate this effect — without ever fully attaining it.

houbb-afif.definition: Chaste love, love that rises through continence
houbb-afif.description: Al-ḥubb al-ʿafīf is chaste love — the love whose restraint is its mark, and which chooses continence not out of coldness but out of high esteem for what it loves. The word derives from ʿiffa, one of the essential components of courtly love (al-ḥubb al-ʿudhrī), for the latter implies precisely continence and chastity. The deep argument, classical in the Arab tradition and then transmitted to the troubadours of Occitania, is the following: what is loved must be held at a distance in order to remain truly loved. Seizure corrupts; respect elevates. The ʿafīf does not renounce out of scruple — he makes room, within his love, for what exceeds the beloved object. But the word has a still higher reach: in the Sufi lexicon, ʿiffa names the restraint of the soul before God — the capacity not to claim His Presence as a right, not to want to seize Him like a prey. To love God chastely is to let Him be God — and not to reduce Him to the idea one forms of Him.

temoin.fr: Let those who do not find the means to marry keep themselves in chastity

houbb-afif.meditation: Before what, within you, does true love ask to wait — without renouncing?
houbb-afif.ouverture: Sufi ʿiffa is the exact attitude of the one who has not yet reached the meeting, and who does not wish to force it. The mystic waits. He loves, he watches, he fasts — but he does not claim. “If you wait,” says a Sufi adage, “He comes. If you claim, you push Him away.” All the nobility of the way is held in this loving patience.

haboub.definition: The one who loves too much, the tender outpourer
haboub.description: Ḥabūb is said of someone who loves too much and who pours out his states of soul easily. It is the intensive form of the lover — the one who does not know how to hold back what he feels, and who speaks of his heart as one speaks of the weather. From the same family comes the most common expression of the Arabic language: yā ḥabībī — my tender friend, my sweet love — which one hears sung throughout the Arab world, from the Maghreb to the Levant. A word of daily use that carries, in its very familiarity, the echo of the whole Arab amorous tradition.

temoin.fr: My tender friend, my sweet love

haboub.meditation: To whom can you say my tender one — without lowering your voice?
haboub.ouverture: The everyday Arabic language addresses beloved beings by the very words it reserves for God in prayer. The proximity of the registers is not a confusion: it is the deep intuition that to love anyone is to train oneself to love the Source of all love.

mouhibb.definition: The lover, the one who loves — and who loves to love
mouhibb.description: Muḥibb is, simply, the lover — the one who loves. The feminine form, muḥibba, and the variant ḥabūba, designate the woman who feels a great tenderness for the man. The word is neutral, common, classical: it is the word grammar chooses when it wishes to designate the lover without particular colouring. But in Turkish, muḥibbī took on an additional sense: it is said of the one who loves to love. It is thus that Suleiman the Magnificent (1494-1566), the sultan-poet, chose this word as his takhalluṣ — pen name. He published all his ghazals under the name of Muhibbî, as if to sign: the one whose love is the principal art.

temoin.fr: I am the lover who never ceases to love
