mouhibb.meditation: Do you love only beings — or do you also love the loving itself?
mouhibb.ouverture: The muḥibb in the strong sense is cousin to the ḥubb al-ḥubb of Ibn ʿArabī: it is the one who has made of loving his soul's craft. To love someone is circumstantial; to love loving is a vocation.

mahboub.definition: The beloved, the one who is loved
mahboub.description: Maḥbūb is the beloved. In the feminine, maḥbūba — which is also commonly used as a first name in the Arab world, and which says, from the very giving of the name to a child, the wish that it be loved all its life. One also says maʿshūqa with the nuance of ʿishq, a more intense desire. The mystical language also knows the words taḥabbub — the act of making oneself dear — and maḥabba, the Sufi station of love. All three are kin: they name the passage between the one who loves and the one who is loved, the reversible exchange that constitutes every true amorous relation.

temoin.fr: O beloved of the heart

mahboub.meditation: Who are you, and for whom — when that person thinks of you in silence?
mahboub.ouverture: The Sufi way shifts the question: the true identity of the believer is not what he does, nor what he knows, but who loves him. When God names someone My Maḥbūb — as the tradition says of the Prophet, designated as Ḥabīb Allāh — it is, at once, the name and the nature of that person that are revealed.

machouq.definition: The strongly coveted beloved, the object of passion
machouq.description: Maʿshūq designates the one who is strongly coveted — the object of ʿishq, of violent desire. In the feminine, maʿshūqa. Where maḥbūb names the beloved in the tender register, maʿshūq names him in the burning register: one does not love a maʿshūq peacefully, one desires him to the point of losing one's reason. It is this word that constitutes the second term of the famous trilogy formed by Ḥāfiẓ and taken up by the whole Persian tradition: ʿishq, ʿāshiq, maʿshūq — love, the lover, the beloved. Three words, one single root, one single movement of the soul. In Persian mysticism, these three terms designate at once the human relation and the divine relation — and end, in high spirituality, by revealing themselves as one single Real under three faces.

temoin.fr: Love, the lover, the beloved

machouq.meditation: When you intensely desire someone — is it him you want, or what he reveals within you?
machouq.ouverture: For Aḥmad Ghazālī (twelfth century), the mystic brother of the great al-Ghazālī, these three words designate but a single movement: love loving itself through all its objects. The distinction between lover, beloved and love is an effect of perspective; at a certain level of the way, it effaces itself.

foutoun.description: Futūn names the enchantment, the capture, the diversion wrought by the beauty of the beloved. The seducer is called fattān or fātin — as well as the one who toys with a woman. To give more relief to their verses, the Arab poets dispose of several synonyms: iftitān, siḥr (magic, sorcery), saḥḥār (charmer), mūnīq (agreeable, pleasing), jaddāb al-qulūb (slayer of hearts). The word has a fearsome theological cousin: fitna, which in the Quran designates at once temptation, discord, and trial — as if amorous seduction, in the Arab imagination, were always brushing against something spiritually dangerous. The beloved woman herself can be named fitna by the theologians. But this same root also yields what God does to the soul in order to purify it.

temoin.fr: Your wealth and your children are only a trial

foutoun.meditation: What captivates you — and what does this captive wish to teach you, about what you do not know of yourself?
foutoun.ouverture: The double meaning of the root f-t-n is read by the Sufis as a teaching: every amorous capture can become a spiritual trial, if one accepts to read in it what it says of oneself. The fitna is not the opposite of the way: it is, at times, its beginning.

taatouf-djinsi.definition: The exchange of amorous courtesies, gentle complicity
taatouf-djinsi.description: Taʿāṭuf jinsī names, in the strict sense, an exchange of amorous courtesies — playful banter, sexual complicity in the light sense, but apprehended from the side of finesse and gentleness. This grace partakes of one of the amorous states gathered under the notion of mulāṭafa — shared tenderness. Not to be confused with ḥubb al-jinsī or ʿishq jinsī, which designate sexual intercourse properly speaking. The taʿāṭuf, for its part, is not the thing itself — it is the atmosphere that surrounds it, the quality of presence of the lovers to one another in their shared intimacy. More than an act, it is a manner: to love the other in all his gestures, and to let him love in all of one's own.

temoin.fr: They exchanged their courtesies in love like two brothers

taatouf-djinsi.meditation: In your loves, what is played out in the manner — that the acts alone do not say?
taatouf-djinsi.ouverture: The Sufis cultivate a similar attention in their relation to God: it is not so much what one does (to pray, to fast, to give) that counts, it is the quality with which one does it. Iḥsān, says the Prophet, is “to worship God as if you saw Him.” Human taʿāṭuf is the school of this manner.

tachbib.definition: The pleasure of singing of women in verse
tachbib.description: Tashbīb names the pleasure of finding oneself in the company of women, of making it known through amorous poetry (the nasīb), and of drawing from it a certain prestige. Several examples are reported to us by the literary annals, in particular by The Book of Songs of Abū l-Faraj al-Iṣfahānī (897-967), a monument of classical Arab culture. In the seventh century, in Medina, the poet Kuthayyir knew glory through the poems he wrote for ʿAzza. In the eleventh, in Andalusia, Ibn Zaydūn (1003-1071) would perhaps not have attained his notoriety without the poetic-erotic correspondence he maintained with the Cordoban princess Wallāda — one of the rare women-poets of her time, and the great lover of the Andalusian evening.

temoin.fr: Whoever falls for the fair one, let him publish his love in verse

tachbib.meditation: What you love — do you know how to give it a form, or do you content yourself with feeling it?
tachbib.ouverture: The great Arabo-Andalusian tradition made of amorous song not only an art but a spiritual discipline: to give form to what one feels is, for the Sufis too, a way of elevating it. To love in silence is noble; to love in forms — poems, letters, dhikr — is more so still.

houbb-sarid.description: Al-ḥubb aṣ-ṣārīd is sincere love. In a social context where the mixing of men and women was traditionally restricted, the treachery of a meeting without a morrow constantly haunted amorous relations. Hence the importance accorded, in classical Arab culture, to the word of honour — kalima — and to the sincerity of commitment — ṣidq. This love is also called al-ḥubb al-khāliṣ — pure love, rid of every ulterior thought. It is the love that says what it will be, and that will be what it says. Unlike the other forms of love that concentrate on the intensity of feeling, the ḥubb aṣ-ṣārīd insists on the word kept: it is not only the heart, it is commitment.

temoin.fr: The sincerity of love is known by fidelity

houbb-sarid.meditation: Are your loves true — that is to say: do they keep what they promised without saying it?
houbb-sarid.ouverture: Ṣidq is one of the great words of Sufism — the inner veracity that makes one be what one says. For Junayd, the accomplished Sufi is the one who says the truth of the heart, and whose heart says the truth of God. To love someone sincerely is therefore a school: one trains oneself there to coincide with what one loves.

houbb-ama.description: Ḥubb aʿmā is blind love — a passion of which you are the vassal. The Arab adage says it with the concise precision of the tradition: “Love makes one blind and mute.” The formula is attributed to Jāḥiẓ (780-869), the great Abbasid prose-writer, a fine observer of human passions. And indeed: to love thus is to cease to see what one loves as it is, and to no longer be able to say what one sees. Blindness and muteness are the two twin symptoms. The aʿmā lover no longer makes the difference between the beloved person and the idea he forms of her, between his desires and reality. In this state, says the tradition, one is rarely a good judge — but perhaps this is what love serves for: that one cease, for a time, to be a judge.

temoin.fr: Love makes one blind and mute

houbb-ama.meditation: What do you no longer see, since you have loved? And why does this blindness, secretly, suit you?
houbb-ama.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish two blindnesses: that of passion, which closes the eyes upon reality; and that of contemplation, which closes the outer eyes in order to open the inner ones. The second is no longer blindness — it is another sight. The first, for its part, is to be traversed without enclosing oneself in it.

houbb-moutrib.description: Ḥubb muṭrib is joyful love. The word is formed on ṭarab, which designates precisely joy, enchantment, the ravishment of the senses — notably in the musical domain. The duality of amorous feeling allows it to be expressed, even among the Arabs (reputed for their melancholic sentimentalism), in a playful and fulfilling manner. This register tends to channel the melancholy of absence and the difficulty of the meeting. The whole tradition of Arabo-Andalusian song, later that of Egyptian music, bears witness to this amorous joy — the one that laughs, that sings, that dances, that makes life possible even when love torments. Ṭarab is the musical antidote to ḥuzn.

temoin.fr: Ṭarab is the nourishment of the soul

houbb-moutrib.meditation: When were you last ravished with joy — not pleased, ravished?
houbb-moutrib.ouverture: Sufi samāʿ — ritualised musical listening — aims precisely to provoke the ṭarab of the soul toward God. It is what the dervishes of Konya practised around Rūmī: to dance, to listen, to sing, until joy becomes stronger than separation. Ṭarab, at this level, is the other name of joyful fanāʾ.

houbb-lothi.description: Ḥubb lūṭī is, in the classical Arabic lexicon, the name given to male homosexual love, or pederasty (liwāṭ). The word comes from the name of the prophet Lūṭ — Lot in the Bible (Genesis XI-XIX), also cited several times in the Quran (VII, XI, XV, XXVI). The people of Lūṭ having given themselves over to practices that the tradition condemns, the expression ended by entering the language as designating this type of attachment. One also says ḥubb khurāsānī. In the eyes of the classical Arabs, indeed, the amorous inclination for boys (liwāṭ) was considered a Khurasani invention, named after the eastern province of Iran situated at the border of Turkmenistan and Afghanistan. Hence the toponym. For love between women, the classical term is siḥāqiyya. These categorisations belong to a precise historical lexicon; they evidently do not exhaust human complexity.

temoin.fr: And Lot, when he said to his people…

houbb-lothi.meditation: What is, in your loves, the part that conforms to no name — and that asks to be thought anew?
houbb-lothi.ouverture: Classical Sufism long debated shāhid-bāzī — the amorous contemplation of beauty incarnate in a human face (often young). Certain masters (Awḥad al-Dīn Kirmānī) made of it a discipline of the way; others (Najm al-Dīn Kubrā) judged it a trap. The debate remains open: can one love the Creator through the face of the creature, without stopping at her? It is one of the live questions of mysticism.

houbb-ibahi.description: Ḥubb ibāḥī names concupiscent love — carnal passion, which is one of the most recurrent themes of Arab erotic literature. The root b-y-ḥ signifies what is licit, permitted — hence ibāḥa, permission. The ibāḥī is the one who permits himself — who assumes his sensuality instead of concealing it. The tales of the Thousand and One Nights, Ibn Falaita (fourteenth century), Shaykh Nefzāwī (fifteenth, author of the Perfumed Meadow) gave colourful illustrations of it. The collective ibāḥiyyūn — the licentious — designates precisely a class of Hijazi poets who, in the sixth and seventh centuries, moved away from the aristocratic norm of courtly love (ʿudhrī) in order to assume the carnal dimension. The great Arab tradition knew how to welcome both faces — modesty and audacity.

temoin.fr: Permitted to you, on the night of the fast, is intimacy with your wives

houbb-ibahi.meditation: What part of your sensuality have you ceased to listen to — to the point that it now expresses itself in noise, not in word?
houbb-ibahi.ouverture: The Quranic verse that permits conjugal intimacy names it by a very direct word (rafath). Classical Islam did not refuse the body: it framed it. Sufism, in its wake, does not combat sensuality — it orients it. To love in the body, and that this body be itself a prayer: such is the way.

ladgh.description: Ladhgh names love-bite, the one that leaves you no respite, at no moment. It is at the edge of carnal love, while being in itself particularly violent. The word has a very close kinship with ladhʿ — the climbing flame — but with this nuance: the bite is what penetrates, whereas the flame is what surrounds. Ladhgh and ladhʿ evoke together the solitude of the lover, the continuous ordeal that grants him no moment of calm. It is no longer only the flame that consumes, it is the animal that bites, that comes back to bite, that does not let go. The preferred poetic expression, for this love, is that of the open wound.

temoin.fr: Love has bitten me like a serpent

ladgh.meditation: What bite does not let go of you — and that, henceforth, is part of you?
ladgh.ouverture: For the Sufis, certain bites are marks of election. All the saints of Islam bore their scar — the one that recalled to them at every instant the Beloved. The ladhgh, in the divine register, is not an ill to heal: it is a sign to honour.

wahl.definition: Sublime love, that petrifies with fear
wahl.description: Al-wahl is sublime, terrifying, transfiguring love. It applies to the beloved one whom one wishes to conquer but of whom one wants him and him alone. It follows that when one sees him, one is paralysed with fear, panicked at the idea of failing, as if petrified by a superior beauty. The same holds for a woman who desires a man, but who is transfixed by his beauty, his power or his charisma. The word says an experience that few modern psychologies still know how to name: the religious feeling of human love, the one in which the other appears to us as a presence so strong that it exceeds our ordinary categories. It is the beauty that makes one tremble, that recalls, in human love, what the theologians name the tremendum of the sacred.

temoin.fr: The heart froze when I saw her

wahl.meditation: Before whom have you been paralysed — and what did this paralysis teach you?
wahl.ouverture: The Sufis recognise in the wahl the human experience closest to the divine hayba — the reverential awe before the Presence. When one glimpses the Real, it is not joy that comes first: it is dread. The way consists in letting this fear blossom into love, without fleeing it.

wajd.description: Wajd is a precious word of the Sufi lexicon, which carries three senses at once. First sense: ardent love, passionate emotion, ecstatic love reserved for the divine Entity. Second sense: love-sorrow, somewhat ecstatic and in certain cases rather metaphysical. Third sense: physical emotion of the most intense kind. The richness of the word lies in an etymological paradox: wajd derives from wajada, to find. Wajdān is nothing less than Existence, the consciousness of being. Yet, says the Sufi tradition, the wajd properly speaking is a lack of love — a state in which the lover does not find the Beloved. And it is precisely this lack that is called “finding.” The apparent contradiction is the teaching: what one seeks, one feels first as absence; and this burning absence is, in reality, the felt presence.

temoin.fr: I found Him in the very ordeal of His absence

wajd.meditation: Have you ever found someone in the very absence you felt?
wajd.ouverture: Ibn ʿArabī made of wajd one of the three key words of his metaphysics of love, alongside mawjūd (the existent) and wujūd (Existence). To love truly is to find the Existent in the very experience of lack — as the night reveals the stars that the day was hiding.

moutbal.definition: Sick with love, stricken in his four modes
moutbal.description: Muṭabbal is sick with love. According to Ibn ʿArabī (1165-1241), the great Muslim mystic and author of the Treatise on Divine Love, such a sickness expresses itself in four distinct modes: amorous melancholy (kamad), sighs (zafarāt), wandering (hiyām) and wasting away (nuḥūl). One also says ṭabāl or taṭabbul to designate the loss of habitual bearings, diversion, rambling. The word therefore says this particular sickness in which the lover is no longer capable of fulfilling his habitual functions — not through idleness, but because a part of himself no longer dwells in the same reality. A sickness that is not, for the old Arab physicians, a simple metaphor: it is diagnosed by the pulse (irregular at the name of the beloved) and treated by the return to the beloved, failing which, at times, death follows.

temoin.fr: Four signs reveal the sick lover: melancholy, sighs, wandering and wasting away
temoin.source: Ibn ʿArabī, Treatise on Divine Love

moutbal.meditation: Which of the four signs — melancholy, sigh, wandering, wasting away — do you recognise within yourself now?
moutbal.ouverture: Ibn ʿArabī adds: “the sickness of love for God is recognised by the same four signs.” There is, in his eyes, but one single love, which passes through creatures and which culminates in the Real. Sickness is not the opposite of spiritual health: it is, at times, its beginning.

maghroum.definition: The captive lover, overcome by his passion
maghroum.description: Maghrūm is said of someone who, smitten by his passion, is a captive lover — the lover dominated by an incurable ill, or else the beloved overcome by the memory of the lover. The word is the passive participle of the root gh-r-m, already encountered in gharām. It is the lover become his debt, who identifies himself with the bond that holds him. Arab popular song — Egyptian in particular — made of this word one of its most poignant refrains. Abdel-Halim Ḥāfeẓ, Farīd al-Aṭrash, beloved singers of the 1940s to the 1970s, could thus cry out “Maghrūm, maghrūm”, literally: “I am in love, I am in love” — but with this precious nuance of accepted captivity that no English equivalent renders.

temoin.fr: I am captive, captive of you

maghroum.meditation: What debt of love do you accept — not by resigning yourself to it, but by embracing it?
maghroum.ouverture: For the Sufis, the passage from suffered gharām to consented maghrūm is one of the thresholds of the path. One can suffer an amorous captivity as a curse; one can also recognise it as a gift. The difference is inward — and it changes everything.

wasb.description: Al-waṣb has the same definition as other words of amorous consumption, but with this nuance: it says perpetual pain, the one that does not let go. Al-waṣīb is the one who complains greatly of this pain — not out of weakness, but because it leaves him no truce to say anything else. The Quran uses the word twice (XVI, 52 and XXXVII, 9), notably to evoke the perpetual chastisement that strikes the unbelievers. This kinship with eschatological vocabulary is not insignificant: it says that the pain of love, in the Arab consciousness, can attain such an intensity that it resembles hell. But it also says its reverse: what can be hell can, in true love, be the antechamber of paradise.

temoin.fr: And to Him is worship due perpetually

wasb.meditation: What perpetual pain do you bear — and what have you learned to make of it?
wasb.ouverture: The Sufi way does not promise the end of pain — it promises its transmutation. The same waṣb that consumes the one who suffers it as a victim, illumines the one who bears it as a servant of the Beloved. The difference is held by a single thread: to whom one addresses, inwardly, the pain one traverses.

jawa.definition: Sovereign emotion, alternation of strength and weakness
jawa.description: Al-jawā is extreme, sovereign emotion, as much in desire and passion as in the sorrow of love. It is the alternation of strength and weakness in the woman or man who loves: one day standing in their passion, the next on the ground in their grief, and always the same intensity that circulates without one knowing any longer whether it is joy or pain. In the perspective of a philosophy of love placed under the control of pain, al-jawā would be the state of inner power the subject feels — but equally the sadness and dejection that are its shadow side. The two are not separable: the same fire can warm or consume, according to the moment, according to the disposition of the one who bears it.

temoin.fr: The jawā is a fire beneath the ash

jawa.meditation: What emotion within you overcomes you one day, and carries you the next — without changing its nature?
jawa.ouverture: The Sufis know that states (aḥwāl) are mobile by nature. One does not establish oneself in the joy of love forever; nor does one remain forever in grief. The sage traverses the states without identifying with any. What remains is not the state — it is the One who experiences them.

la-j.definition: The strong contrition of love, the necessary ill
la-j.description: Al-laʿj names a state of strong contrition, a necessary ill. The word carries a dimension difficult to translate: it is at once the burning of regret and the conviction that this regret is just. It is probable that, by way of the root l-ʿ-j, the word maintains a relation with al-iktiʾāb — love-sorrow. The word also says something of the acute consciousness of what one has done, or of what one has not done. It is not simple grief: it is the grief that knows why it is there, that has looked the past in the face, that does not flee responsibility. In this respect, the laʿj is a moral feeling as much as an amorous one — that of the lover who deems himself, in his own conscience, accountable for what has come undone.

temoin.fr: The laʿj devours the heart, truly

la-j.meditation: What regret do you bear, of which you know it is just, even if it is unbearable?
la-j.ouverture: The Sufis name tawba the conversion through regret. The laʿj is the antechamber of tawba: one turns toward the Light only when one has truly looked at the shadow. True contrition is not a depression — it is a lucidity that prepares a movement.

danf.definition: The incurable ill, love as dependency
danf.description: Danaf applies to a person stricken by an incurable ill (maraḍ). In the case of the lover, such a sickness evokes the habituation of the one who is subjected to a drug, prevented from emancipating himself, or even from combating. Love, at this stage, is no longer a feeling one chooses or undergoes — it is an organic dependency from which one no longer emerges. Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (1272-1350), the great theologian and author of the Garden of Lovers, seems to hesitate to define this term. He remarks that it was abandoned by the Ancients, at least in the amorous domain, but that it is much in favour among his contemporaries. In Chronicles on Women, the modern scholar Omar Ridha Kaḥḥāla uses rather the word aḍ-ḍayf. The lexicon evolves; the thing itself remains.

temoin.fr: Stricken by love with no hope of healing

danf.meditation: What amorous dependency do you accept — no longer seeking to heal?
danf.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish the dependencies that enslave (to the creature) and the dependencies that liberate (to the Creator). Human danaf is generally the first; mystical danaf, the second. Yet one must have experienced the first in order to recognise the second.

chajw.definition: Difficult love, alternation of tears and sadness
chajw.description: Shajw names a difficult love in which tears (bukāʾ) and sadness (ḥuzn) alternate. Such a state is characterised by a tightened throat and a welling of tears. A sad lover is called shajīn; the woman in love who does not have what she desires is said to be shajiyatun. In Persian, one says of a tightened heart that it is del-tang — literally, the narrow heart. The shajw is cousin to ḥuzn and to lahf, with this nuance: it insists on the oscillation, on the fact that the pain comes and goes in waves — not a flat sea, but a sea agitated from which one never knows what the next wave will bring. This instability is precisely what makes the difficulty of this love: one cannot settle into it, one must traverse it at each new instant.

temoin.fr: Love has tightened my heart with a pain that does not cease

chajw.meditation: What oscillation do you bear, without being able to rest on either side?
chajw.ouverture: The Sufis name ḥāl the passing states of the soul, and maqām the stable stations. The shajw is a ḥāl: it comes, it goes. All wisdom consists in not confusing it with a station — in not dwelling where one is only passing through.

dhouboul.description: Dhubūl names consumption, the physical decay of the lover who no longer eats, no longer sleeps, who melts away little by little. Ibn ʿArabī, in his Treatise on Divine Love, includes dhubūl in the list of amorous states: “the ardent desire of love (shawq), amorous domination (gharām), distraught love (hiyām), the pain of love (kalāf), tears (bakāʾ), sadness (ḥuzn), the wound of love (kabd), consumption (dhubūl), languor (inkisār) — and other similar states.” The image is that of the flower that withers on its stem — that does not die violently, but that comes undone slowly, losing its colour, its firmness, its beauty. Dhabala is precisely the verb that says this progressive withering. The dhābil lover is not dead: he is in the process of effacing himself, like a drawing one has not re-fixed.

temoin.fr: The body has withered as the flower withers

dhouboul.meditation: What, within you, is in the process of withering — and that you have not yet decided to water?
dhouboul.ouverture: The dhubūl is, for the Sufis, one of the signs that human love has reached its limit: what no longer nourishes you is precisely what reveals to you your true hunger. One cannot indefinitely nourish a flower with what dries it out. To recognise the dhubūl is the first act of the return to the Source.

amid.definition: The one whom love has made so weak he can no longer rise
amid.description: ʿAmīd is weakened by love. The word applies to someone who is too sick — dāʾ, maraḍ — to be able to rise by himself. It is the image of the column that leans, of the pillar that no longer stands: ʿamūd, in the root, is precisely the column. Ibn al-Fāriḍ (1181-1235), the great mystical poet of Cairo, is said to have stated: “Love — its rest is a fatigue, its beginning a sickness, and its end death. But for me, death by love is a life.” The whole secret of the ʿamīd is in this last sentence: death by love is a life. The one who can no longer rise by himself is precisely the one who discovers that he had no need to hold himself up alone.

temoin.fr: Love — its death through Your love is eternity

amid.meditation: What weakness within you accepts to no longer rise alone — and has discovered, in this abandonment, something other than a defeat?
amid.ouverture: The Sufi way knows this station: the mystical ʿamīd is the one who has renounced holding himself up by himself, and who discovers that the Presence holds him — otherwise, more durably than his own strength did. What resembles a fall is, at times, the beginning of a flight.

inkisar.definition: Languor, the breaking of the amorous heart
inkisar.description: Inkisār applies to the languor that accompanies amorous feeling — to its breaking, to its shattering. The root k-s-r is very precise: kasara is to break. Inkisār, in the passive voice: to be broken. The lover's heart is maksūr, broken — not in the sense that it would have ceased to function, but in the sense that what held it in one single piece has come undone. Ibn ʿArabī includes inkisār in his canonical list of amorous states, alongside dhubūl and kabd (the wound of the liver, the organ that is the seat of the emotions in ancient Arabic). But the Sufi way does not depreciate the broken heart: “a broken heart,” says a Sufi adage, “is an open heart.” It is through its breaks that the light enters.

temoin.fr: A broken heart is worth more than a proud heart

inkisar.meditation: Through what break within you does the light now enter?
inkisar.ouverture: Rūmī said it in a verse become universal: “The wound is the place through which the light enters.” The Sufis do not seek to avoid inkisār — they welcome it, because they know that no Presence enters an intact heart. The whole heart has not yet loved.

yatm.description: Al-yatm signifies literally the fact of being abandoned, of becoming an orphan. Applied to love, it evokes the violent separation, the rending, the obsession with the absent other who envelops, stuns and enthralls without restraint. The kinship with tatayyum — love-adoration — is no etymological accident: it is the same root. The abandoned lover becomes an orphan of the beloved; and it is precisely this condition of orphan that pushes him to adore, that is to say to depend absolutely on what he has lost. The word says this continuity: separation creates adoration; adoration is the mode of presence of the absent beloved. When someone dies within us, one learns, by counter-blow, what they were for us.

temoin.fr: Did He not find you an orphan, and shelter you?

yatm.meditation: Of whom have you become an orphan — and what did you adore, from that point on, in their place?
yatm.ouverture: The Quranic verse addressed to the Prophet reveals a deep dialectic: to become an orphan is, at times, the invitation to be sheltered elsewhere. Every grave human loss hollows within us a space that only the Real can inhabit. The yatm is not a terminus — it is a threshold.

chawq.definition: Intense desire, the transport toward the beloved
chawq.description: Shawq names the amorous transport, whether by thought or by heart (qalb) — the “love of covetousness,” as Massignon translated it. Lahīb ash-shawq designates the devouring passion and the nostalgia for the absent love. Mushtāq — literally the desiring one — designates the person who feels an impulse toward the meeting. In mysticism, shawq is one of the key words: it is the active desire for the divine meeting, the surge that pushes the soul upward, the movement by which the lover can no longer bear the distance. A letter attributed to Junayd says: “The shawq is the brazier that God lights in the hearts of His friends so that they may return to Him.”

temoin.fr: I have had for You a shawq that no water extinguishes

chawq.meditation: What desire, within you, is extinguished by no water?
chawq.ouverture: Ibn ʿArabī distinguishes the shawq (which can be extinguished in the meeting) and the ishtiyāq (which remains even in the meeting). The most advanced souls of the path know a shawq that does not subside: the more they find, the more they desire. To love God, at this level, is to have a desire perpetually renewed.

ichtiyaq.definition: The nostalgia for the distant love, the desire that remains
ichtiyaq.description: According to certain grammarians, the ishtiyāq is more dense than the shawq, but the traditional school thinks the contrary. It is also said that the ishtiyāq concerns the absent beloved, whereas the shawq addresses itself as much to the absent as to the present person. Tashāwaqa: to desire someone strongly, to think of him, to await him. In Sufi lexicography, this word designates precisely the nostalgia for a distant, exiled, inaccessible love. It belongs to the rare family of feelings in which the lack is part of the enjoyment: one loves, in part, because the beloved is not there. This ambiguity is central in mysticism: the apparent absence of God is not a lack to be filled — it is a mode of His presence.

temoin.fr: My ishtiyāq toward You is my very existence

ichtiyaq.meditation: What lack, within you, does not ask to be filled — but to be inhabited?
ichtiyaq.ouverture: For the Sufis, the ishtiyāq is, paradoxically, the proof of the meeting, not its contrary. One feels a lack only of what one has known. The more the soul has tasted the Presence, the more its absence hollows. The saint, at a certain level, lives in a perpetual ishtiyāq — not as a suffering but as the normal breathing of his love.

law-a.definition: The consumption of love, the fever of the torn heart
law-a.description: Lawʿa is the consumption of love, the fever of a heart torn by passion. One also says al-walāʾ and al-wulūʾ — amorous enthusiasm. The impassioned one is called mawlūʾ (in the feminine: mawlūʾa). A verse of the poet ʿUmar (seventh century) says it with a striking precision: he sings of a beloved whose adorned beauty, blended perfume and gazelle-like neck have ravished his soul utterly. The lawʿa is the fever lit by this precise beauty, this particular detail — the gazelle's neck that consumes the poet's heart.

temoin.fr:
By a face adorned with a blended perfume…
she has ravished my soul

law-a.meditation: What detail consumed you — a perfume, a neck, a light upon the skin?
law-a.ouverture: The Sufis hold that every detail of beauty is a window upon the Real. When the poet burns for the neck of a gazelle, it is not the neck alone that burns him — it is what that neck lets pass through. To love thus is to learn to traverse what one loves in order to reach what loves through us.

qalb.definition: The heart — metaphor and organ of love
qalb.description: Qalb — My Heart — is used as a metaphor to designate love. Yā qalbī — O my heart! — whose seat is, in Arab culture, situated in the heart itself. The word is above all in use in the sentimental song, with at times a shifted equivalence toward fuʾād (liver) and kabidah, kabdatī — the latter reserved rather for maternal love. Moreover, one also says yā ʿaynī — “O the pupil of my eyes.” Al-Jāḥiẓ (780-869) signals an Arab expression: “The eye of love — hawā — is not sincere.” The expression muqlat ʿaynī, the “pupil of my eyes,” recurs in numerous literary texts. The heart, the eye, the liver: three metaphorical organs by which the Arabic language locates precisely where, in the body, one loves.

temoin.fr: Therein is a reminder for whoever possesses a heart

qalb.meditation: Do you love with your heart — or with what you take to be your heart?
qalb.ouverture: For the Sufis, the qalb is not the physical organ: it is the subtle centre of the being, the place where the human meets the divine. The root q-l-b signifies turning over — the heart is precisely what turns over, what changes, what can be turned toward or away. Muqallib al-qulūb — the One who turns the hearts over — is one of the names of God in the tradition.

khilaba.description: Khilāba names deceitful love. The word is so named because it is situated between the heart and the abdominal cavity: the heart acquiesces and says yes, but, deceitful, the dark abdominal cavity refuses and says no. It is the love that speaks in two opposed voices within the same body. The khullāb is the name given to the cloud that promises a few drops of rain, but that suddenly thinks better of it and disappears into the haze. An admirable image of false love: it announces, it makes one hope, and it gives nothing. Takhallub is a word of the ancient nasīb that designates the attitude of coquetry, of mocking ostentation and of false promise in the woman. Not to be confused with khabl, which is dementia and not deception.

temoin.fr: The khilāba is a cloud without rain

khilaba.meditation: What rainless clouds do you still await — when you know they will give nothing?
khilaba.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish the promises of the nafs (the ordinary soul, which lies) and the promises of the rūḥ (the spirit, which does not lie). The khilāba is typical of the nafs: it makes glimmer what it does not have. To learn to recognise the false rains is one of the first acts of spiritual discernment.

balabil.definition: The thoughts of love, the obsessions, the reveries
balabil.description: Balābīl — in the singular balbala — names every thought linked to love, its reminiscences, its obsessions and its phantasmagorias. It is the cloud of thoughts that whirl in the lover's heart and that never leave him — not as an ordered procession, but as an agitated swarm. The kinship with bulbul — the nightingale, the emblematic bird of Persian and Arabic poetry — is doubtless no etymological accident. As the nightingale sings distractedly to the rose that does not answer it, the balābīl thoughts turn around the absent beloved, without being able to fall silent. The word itself evokes oscillation: it is a word that stammers, that repeats itself, that does not know how to be a single one.

temoin.fr: The thoughts of love do not subside

balabil.meditation: What swarm of thoughts keeps you awake — and what, conversely, does the inability to chase them away teach you?
balabil.ouverture: The practice of dhikr is not a combat against the balābīl: it is their reorientation. Rather than chasing away the thoughts (impossible), the Sufi aligns them upon the divine Name. All the thoughts then become, without ceasing to be numerous, a single thought multiplied.

sadamou.description: As-sadam names a love sanctioned by regret (nadām) and by sorrow (ḥuzn). The term is built on a triliteral base: sā-di-mūn, like nā-di-mūn (those who regret). The formal kinship says the kinship of meaning: al-sadam is love lived under the sign of regret, of which each memory is tainted by the consciousness of having missed something. It is the love that gazes upon itself in the past, that replays the scenes to seek there the instant when everything tipped over. This replaying is not innocent: it gnaws. Sadim is therefore said of the one who is no longer wholly in the present, because a part of himself turns endlessly around a point already passed.

temoin.fr: A regret does not resurrect what is past

sadamou.meditation: What do you still replay — when you know that no return backward is possible?
sadamou.ouverture: Sufi tawba — the conversion through regret — is the antidote to sadam. Instead of turning endlessly around the past, one uses it as a pivot to turn toward the Presence. True regret does not resurrect the past: it serves the present.

ghamarat.description: Ghamarāt is the intensive plural of ghamrah, and designates love seen as an abyss, a drowning, an annihilation (maḥw, fanāʾ). The Quran employs the word with a singular force: “Leave them, for a time, in their drowning — fī ghamratihim — until a certain moment” (XXIII, 54). The word says that love can be a water so deep that one no longer rises from it. But unlike ordinary drowning — fatal — the amorous ghamrah is, in mysticism, the threshold of another mode of life. Ghamara, in Arabic, also signifies to cover entirely — and it is from this total covering that a new birth can be born. The lover who has let himself be covered is no longer the one who was struggling at the surface.

temoin.fr: Leave them in their drowning until a certain time

ghamarat.meditation: In what water are you letting yourself be covered — and what do you await, beneath the surface?
ghamarat.ouverture: The Sufis made of the ghamrah the very image of fanāʾ: one does not annihilate oneself through effort, one lets oneself be covered. The spiritual gesture is not the relentless swim — it is the abandonment to the depth. And it is in this abandonment, paradoxically, that one emerges at a level one would never have reached by struggling.

rassis.description: Rassīs names eternal love, durable love. Certain poets established a comparison they nevertheless knew to be inexact between the strength of this love, solid and stable, and the fever with its devastating effect: the one is durable, the other is momentary. The rassīs is precisely what remains when all the fevers have passed. The root r-s-s is interesting: rassa signifies to dig, to bury, to plant deeply. Ar-rassīs is what is rooted — what is not found at the surface, what resists the seasons. It is the love of long fidelities, of companionships that last, of the great maḥabba that have traversed the ordeals without breaking. In mysticism, it is the name of the love of God rooted in the soul — the one that no longer depends on passing states, that remains even when spiritual dryness strikes.

temoin.fr: A rooted love that does not change

rassis.meditation: What love, within you, has become rooted — to the point that no season can any longer uproot it?
rassis.ouverture: For the Sufis, the rassīs is the sign that love has reached the station of stable maḥabba. It is no longer an emotion that comes and goes — it is a trait of character, a quality of being, what the ancients called a virtue. To love thus is to be loving.

kalaf.description: Kalaf is an intense and demanding love. Al-kalif is the one who is smitten with love — kalifa, kilfān. The root k-l-f has a double sense: on one side, the love that imposes itself (kalifa = to be taken by love); on the other, obligation, task (taklīf = religious obligation). This double sense is rich: to love thus is to impose upon oneself a burden one had not chosen. The kalif lover did not will this love — he received it as a charge, and he must now bear it. It is love as duty, but a duty that comes from within, that has not been decreed by an external law. Ibn ʿArabī classes the kalaf among the true states of love, alongside shawq, gharām, hiyām.

temoin.fr: I became smitten with him with an unbearable love

kalaf.meditation: What love has been imposed upon you — not by a decision but as a charge to bear?
kalaf.ouverture: The Sufis read the kalaf through the notion of taklīf: religious obligation. To love God is, in the highest perspective, at once the grace received (gratuitous gift) and the taklīf assumed (consented charge). One does not go without the other: grace is tasted only in proportion as one accepts the charge.

hayajan.description: Hayajān al-ḥubb is love-passion — al-ḥubb al-hāʾij. It is like a great river into which all the impetuous torrents pour their power. The river contains them all, without leaving its bed — unless the emotion is at its height. Hence this love-overflowing (hayajān) that nothing resists, neither the distance from the beloved (buʿd, infiṣāl), nor the serene inclination (mayl), nor even the most ardent desires (ashwāq, malaḏḏāt). The hayajān is love at its tipping point: one has long held on, then something gives way, and everything pours out. The river overflows. And what has poured out can no longer return to the bed that contained it.

temoin.fr: Love has unleashed within me its course

hayajan.meditation: What inner torrent are you waiting to let out — and what do you fear of this overflowing?
hayajan.ouverture: The Sufis know that the way encounters, at certain stations, necessary overflowings. One cannot indefinitely contain what wishes to come out. Wisdom consists in discerning the fruitful overflowings (which reveal) from the destructive ones (which ravage). The criterion, says Junayd, is simple: “if it draws nearer to God, let it go; if it draws away, hold it back.”

mihnah.definition: The ordeal of love, the tribulation
mihnah.description: Miḥna signifies ordeal. The word is said in general of all that obsesses and saddens, and concerns particularly amorous feeling, which is often an additional torment for the human being. The root m-ḥ-n has a judicial reach: it is also the word for interrogation, examination, putting to the test. Love as trial, the beloved as judge. In the history of Islam, the Miḥna with a capital letter designates the great theological inquisition of the ninth century, in which the Abbasid caliph compelled the scholars to recognise certain doctrines. Several refused, even under torture. The kinship with the amorous ordeal is less strange than it appears: to love truly is also to be interrogated to the very depths, and to have to answer for it with one's life.

temoin.fr: The lover is put to the test by his very love

mihnah.meditation: What amorous ordeal are you traversing, that is also an interrogation about who you are?
mihnah.ouverture: For the Sufis, the ordeal of love is an obligatory passage of the way. Rābiʿa said: “Whom Allah loves, He tries.” The logic is rigorous: one does not know what one loves until one has been tried upon that love. The miḥna is not a punishment — it is a revealer.

chajan.definition: The object of love, in its ambivalence
chajan.description: Shajan names the object of love in itself, with its happy and unhappy ambivalence. It is the beloved considered not as a person, but as the source of the feeling — what, in him, makes at once the joy and the pain of the one who loves him. Not to be confused with shajw (the difficult love alternating tears and sadness), although their neuralgic point remains identical. The plural, shujūn, designates the ramifications of love — the numerous thoughts, the returns of the heart, the associations that make a single beloved become, in the lover's mind, a whole forest of memories, desires and griefs. Al-ḥadīth dhū shujūn is an adage: “conversation has its ramifications” — one begins by speaking of one thing, one ends by speaking of another, one no longer knows how one arrived there.

temoin.fr: Speech has its ramifications

chajan.meditation: What beloved has, within you, become a forest — where every path leads back to him?
chajan.ouverture: For Ibn ʿArabī, every true beloved has this quality of shajan: he is no longer a face, he has become a matrix of associations. When one has loved deeply, the slightest street, the slightest perfume, the slightest word leads back to the beloved. The whole world becomes his echo. It is, at a higher level, what the mystic lives with God: everything recalls Him.

ghalilah.description: Ghalīla derives from ighlāl and ghulla, which signify intense thirst, to be parched with. One of the acceptations of the term is used to qualify the one who undergoes a powerful desire or an inextinguishable hatred. Hatred and desire are very near to one another, says Arab wisdom, when lovers cross a critical threshold in their relation. This kinship is not a paradox: it is a law of passion. The most opposed feelings — desire and hatred, attraction and repulsion, devotion and resentment — share the same intensity. The opposite of love is not hatred: it is indifference. As long as there is ghalīla, in one direction or the other, there is a bond. Love-hatred is, paradoxically, still a love.

temoin.fr: Love and hatred are on the side of the near

ghalilah.meditation: What intensity do you bear — that could tip in one direction or the other, without changing its strength?
ghalilah.ouverture: The Sufis hold that the nafs does not distinguish love and hatred — it distinguishes only what concerns me and what is indifferent to me. It is in spiritual work that one learns to transmute hatred into compassion without losing anything of the intensity. “Love your enemies,” says the Gospel: it is not a moral, it is an alchemy.

miqa.description: Al-miqa is formed on wāmiqa, yamiqu and designates the love of full enjoyment. Ad-Daylamī (950-1030), the great Sufi theoretician of love, left this precious word: “To a tender heart, a delicate suffering.” The word says this love that is at once full (enjoyment is not a detour, it is the very reality of the experience) and tender (it is not a matter of violent ecstasy). It is the love of long happy fidelities, where pain and joy are no longer contradictory: the pain is delicate, the joy is tranquil. Daylamī, who wrote the Treatise of Mystical Love, knew how to class this state among the most precious of the spiritual path — the one in which one has ceased to oppose pleasure and suffering, because one has accepted them both.

temoin.fr: To a tender heart, a delicate suffering

miqa.meditation: Do you know a love in which pain and joy have the same fineness — without contradiction?
miqa.ouverture: For Daylamī, the al-miqa is the goal of the amorous path: a quality of presence in which nothing is any longer rough, in which all the savours have become subtle. It is the love of the great spiritual ages — the one that no longer cries out, that no longer bursts forth, but that remains and that radiates.

da-moukhamir.definition: The intoxicated pain, the love that swells the sails
da-moukhamir.description: Ad-dāʾ al-mukhāmir signifies literally “the intoxicated pain.” The expression applies to the love that swells and that fills the sails of passion. The root kh-m-r is that of khamr, wine; mukhāmir evokes fermentation, what rises by itself — like the dough that leavens, like the must that becomes wine. Majnūn left this verse that the whole tradition has retained: Passing by the house, the house of Laylā, I kiss this wall, that other, and that one there. Is it from loving the walls that you lose your reason? — Not the walls, my heart: the people of the house.

temoin.fr:
I pass before the house, the house of Laylā —
I kiss this wall, that other, and that one there.
Is it from loving the walls that you lose your reason?
— Not the walls: the people of the house.

da-moukhamir.meditation: What pain is fermenting within you, becoming little by little an intoxication?
da-moukhamir.ouverture: The image is central in mysticism: true love ferments. It does not arrive ready-made — it needs time, heat, darkness. Amorous pain is not a stable state: it is a process that transforms sugar into wine, grief into wisdom. Yet one must let time do its work.

tabarih.definition: The dry halt of love, the clean rupture
tabarih.description: According to Ibn Qayyim al-Jawziyya (fourteenth century), author of the Garden of Lovers (Rawḍat al-muḥibbīn), tabārīh signifies the dry halt of love. Other authors write tabārīj, with the same signification. The root b-r-ḥ evokes the leaving, the abandoning; but also the torment, the pain. The dry halt is therefore not an appeasement — it is a rupture that leaves the lover in suspense, without transition, without preparation. Love has not diminished; it has been cut off. The intensive form (ta-fāʿīl) says the multiplicity of these ruptures: love does not stop once, it re-stops at each attempt at return. And each halt is a death in miniature.

temoin.fr: Love departed all at once and said nothing

tabarih.meditation: What love stopped within you without transition — and what did you do with the silence that followed?
tabarih.ouverture: The Sufis distinguish the endings that destroy and the endings that liberate. Not all ruptures resemble one another. Some are, in spiritual time, acts of grace: they remove what the soul could no longer free itself from. The tabārīh can be so: not a drama, but a deliverance — yet one must have the patience to wait for it to reveal itself as such.

araq.definition: The sickness of love, the insomnia of passion
araq.description: Araq names the sickness of love — the state of the one who keeps watch through entire nights thinking of his love. The derived word tarqīq — delicacy, refinement — could correspond, says Massignon, to the love of ephebes and would therefore have a homosexual vocation. Certain poems of Abū Nuwās (762-813), the great bacchic and libertine poet of Baghdad, belong to the genre of tarqīq. Another synonym: raqīq. The word is cousin to suhd — the insomnia of love — but with this nuance: the araq insists on the sickness, on the fact that the lover does not sleep because he suffers. The suhd is an active watch (one keeps watch); the araq is a suffered watch (one cannot fall asleep). The one is intentional, the other is involuntary.

temoin.fr: Love has given me insomnia: I no longer sleep

araq.meditation: What thought prevents you from sleeping, without your having invited it to keep watch with you?
araq.ouverture: The Sufis know that insomnia can be a badly wrapped gift: God at times wakes His friends in the night so that they may meet Him in the silence that sleep was hiding from them. If the watch is suffered, says the way, transform it into a consented watch. Instead of turning in bed thinking of the beloved, rise and invoke the Name.

halak.description: Halāk names love-perdition, love as finitude, destruction. It happens that, through his immoderate desire (muhlika, “fatal love”), the lover puts his life in peril, loves until the extinction of his voice, dries up and renders his last breath. The Arab tradition long commented on the death of lovers: the Banū ʿUdhrah (the legendary Yemeni tribe) died when they loved, and the tradition often understood this literally. Ibn Ḥazm recounts in the Ring of the Dove several attested cases of lovers dead of grief. For the Arab physicians — al-Rāzī, Ibn Sīnā — this halāk was not a metaphor: it was a cause of mortality in the same way as fever.

temoin.fr: We are a people who die when we love
temoin.source: Adage attributed to the Banū ʿUdhrah, 7th century

halak.meditation: Is there, within you, a love for which you would be ready to die — or only to survive?
halak.ouverture: For the Sufis, to die of human love is a stage still interior to the path. The ultimate halāk is to die of love for God, and it is precisely this that Ibn al-Fāriḍ celebrates: “Love — its beginning is a sickness, its rest a fatigue, its end death. But for me, death by love is a life.”

houbb-hajir.definition: The love of separation, love founded upon exile
houbb-hajir.description: Al-ḥubb al-hājir derives from hijr — distancing, amorous separation, absence. It is a love founded upon the exile of one of the partners, sometimes of both. It is opposed to waṣl and to ḥabl, which evoke union with the beloved or the meeting with the lover. But the root h-j-r has a central historical resonance for Islam: it is the same root that yields hijra, the Hegira — the emigration of the Prophet from Mecca to Medina in 622, which founds the Muslim calendar. Every Muslim therefore understands, in hijr, the double dimension: the separation from a beloved place is also, at times, the founding act of a new life. Amorous separation is not only the end of a love — it can be the beginning of something else.

temoin.fr: Those who emigrated for God after having suffered injustice…

houbb-hajir.meditation: Of what separation have you made — without knowing it — the beginning of something else?
houbb-hajir.ouverture: For the Sufis, every hijra is at once exterior (to leave a place) and interior (to leave a part of oneself). Amorous separation, lived spiritually, is no longer only a loss — it is a passage. “Do not weep for the one who has departed,” says a ḥadīth, “be grateful for what this departure reveals.” The pain of absence is, at a certain level, already the first glimmer of the coming meeting.

sabwa.definition: The strong inclination, the attraction for what refuses itself
sabwa.description: Ṣabwa names a strong inclination for something, and constitutes, in many regards, a synonym of shawq. But in one of its acceptations, this notion describes with precision the lover who is attracted either by a person younger than himself — a ṣabiyya, a young girl — or by a person who refuses herself. In both cases, the word says this attraction for what remains inaccessible, through youth or through evasion. In the Quranic sura that bears this name, Joseph the chaste speaks to God: “My Lord! Prison seems preferable to me than the sin to which they incite me. But if You do not turn their ruses away from me, I will yield (asbū) and I will be among the ignorant.” (XII, 33). Asbū, I will incline, is of the root ṣabā — the same as ṣabwa. In the eleventh century, in Andalusia, the poets Abū ʿĀmir ibn Maslama and Ibn Khafāja used two expressions become famous: malik aṣ-ṣibā, the king of love, and dāʿī aṣ-ṣibā, the angel who calls to love.

temoin.fr: If You do not turn their ruses away from me, I will incline toward them

sabwa.meditation: Toward what do you incline, because it refuses itself — and that you take this refusal for a call?
sabwa.ouverture: The Sufis see in the Quranic ṣabwa of Joseph a precious parable: to recognise the inclination before it becomes an act, and to name it — that is already half of the way. What refuses itself to you can be what protects you — yet one must accept not to seize.

houbb-ghayour.definition: Jealous love, the possessiveness of passion
houbb-ghayour.description: Al-ḥubb al-ghayūr is jealous, possessive love. The ardour of the lover is seen at times in his possessiveness (ḥiyāza, iḥtiyāẓ) — but jealousy (ghīra) cannot be substituted for the freedom of the beloved. This contradiction is at the heart of passion: to love, to tame one's jealousy, and to accept the freedom of the other. In Islam and in the classical Arab world, jealousy is a positive feeling, an advanced “station” in asceticism, which only a few Sufis have attained. Among them, Shiblī (861-945), the mystic of Baghdad, but before him, the Prophet himself had declared that he was jealous. The ḥadīth reports these words: “Allah is jealous, and the believer is jealous. The jealousy of Allah is that a believer should do what He has forbidden.” The divine ghīra is what makes it so that God does not accept that the soul share His love with another master. To love truly is to want the other wholly — but without possessing him, because one has understood that the freedom of the other is inseparable from his love.

temoin.fr: God is jealous

houbb-ghayour.meditation: How do you bear the freedom of those you love — the freedom that exceeds you, the freedom that could leave you?
houbb-ghayour.ouverture: The Sufi ghīra is the attribute of the soul that has understood that the Beloved is the Unique and that no longer bears any veil. Not the jealousy that possesses the creature; the jealousy that wishes that nothing interpose itself between the soul and the Presence. “Be jealous of My love,” says the divine voice to the mystic. “Let no one enter between us, not even yourself.”

la-aj.definition: The overcome lover, abandoned by the beloved
la-aj.description: Al-lāʿij is said of someone who feels very strongly amorous emotion and the contrition that accompanies it, above all in the case of abandonment by the beloved person. The word derives from the same root as al-laʿj (the state of strong contrition, the necessary ill), but it insists on the person of the overcome lover more than on the state itself. Where the laʿj names the burning of regret as an experience, the lāʿij names the one who bears it — the one whose whole silhouette says the abandonment, whose every gesture recalls what he has lost. It is the abandoned lover as a figure of Arab passion: Majnūn after the definitive departure of Laylā, Ibn Zaydūn after the rupture with Wallāda, and with them all those whom the tradition counts among the shuhadāʾ al-ḥubb, the martyrs of love. The Arabic language thus constructs, by the simple play of derivation, a whole typology of amorous suffering: one has not only a state (laʿj), one has also the one who incarnates it (lāʿij) and the movement that engenders it (laʿaja). Each suffering has its complete name — its verb, its state, its subject.

temoin.fr: The lāʿij of love is like the one who burns with a slow fire

la-aj.meditation: Of whom are you, at this precise moment, the lāʿij — the overcome one who bears the mark of absence?
la-aj.ouverture: The Sufis made of the figure of the divine lāʿij the archetype of the mystic tried by the apparent absence of God. Yaḥyā ibn Muʿādh al-Rāzī (ninth century) said: “The servant abandoned by God is more loved by God than the one He caresses. For the former knows, and the other believes he knows.” Abandonment is, at a certain level of the way, a disguised visitation.
